# Owner interaction preferences

Personal preferences from the repo owner for how the assistant should interact — tone, summary style, end-of-turn conventions, how results and decisions are surfaced, and the phrases the owner uses to trigger defined commands. These are **not** project conventions (those live in the [packs/](../packs/) and [skills/](../skills/) corpus); they're how the owner likes to be worked with. Where a preference triggers a command whose mechanics are a project convention, the mechanics stay in their own doc and the entry here just owns the trigger phrase.

## Preferences

- **When there's nothing left to do, say so boldly at the end of the conversation.** Don't bury a "this is complete / I'm blocked / nothing further to do" behind hedging — make the terminal state unmistakable. **Set it apart visually so it catches the eye:** put the terminal state — or, when work remains, the list of open/outstanding items — in its own callout (a Markdown blockquote box, e.g. `> ✅ All done` or `> ⚠️ Still open: …`), not trailing prose the owner has to scan for. One or the other always gets the box: the done-signal when nothing remains, the open-items list when something does.
- **"LGTM"** is the owner's phrase for the merge-to-main command: it asks for the change in front of the owner to be merged into `main`. It's an owner-triggered command; the default mechanics live in the `merge-to-main` skill ([../skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md](../skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md)) (squash, gate on CI only when the repo has it), and a project that diverges overrides them via a bespoke merge-policy file it names in its own `CLAUDE.md`.
- **"bump version"** is the owner's phrase for raising a project's version (a minor bump by default). It's an owner-triggered command; the mechanics — which files to edit and how a release follows — live in the consuming project's workflow doc.

attachment.stdout:
# Owner interaction preferences

Personal preferences from the repo owner for how the assistant should interact — tone, summary style, end-of-turn conventions, how results and decisions are surfaced, and the phrases the owner uses to trigger defined commands. These are **not** project conventions (those live in the [packs/](../packs/) and [skills/](../skills/) corpus); they're how the owner likes to be worked with. Where a preference triggers a command whose mechanics are a project convention, the mechanics stay in their own doc and the entry here just owns the trigger phrase.

## Preferences

- **When there's nothing left to do, say so boldly at the end of the conversation.** Don't bury a "this is complete / I'm blocked / nothing further to do" behind hedging — make the terminal state unmistakable. **Set it apart visually so it catches the eye:** put the terminal state — or, when work remains, the list of open/outstanding items — in its own callout (a Markdown blockquote box, e.g. `> ✅ All done` or `> ⚠️ Still open: …`), not trailing prose the owner has to scan for. One or the other always gets the box: the done-signal when nothing remains, the open-items list when something does.
- **"LGTM"** is the owner's phrase for the merge-to-main command: it asks for the change in front of the owner to be merged into `main`. It's an owner-triggered command; the default mechanics live in the `merge-to-main` skill ([../skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md](../skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md)) (squash, gate on CI only when the repo has it), and a project that diverges overrides them via a bespoke merge-policy file it names in its own `CLAUDE.md`.
- **"bump version"** is the owner's phrase for raising a project's version (a minor bump by default). It's an owner-triggered command; the mechanics — which files to edit and how a release follows — live in the consuming project's workflow doc.
- **"learned lessons"** (also **"lessons learned"**) is the owner's phrase to trigger a retrospective pass over the current conversation — an owner-triggered command the owner invokes on demand. The method — what clears the bar, deduping, the separate efficiency pass, "no new lessons" as a valid and common outcome, and routing each capture — lives in [extracting-lessons.md](../skills/lessons-learned/extracting-lessons.md). Deliver any capture as a change for review — commit on a branch and open a PR, never push straight to `main` (it needs my separate merge approval).
- **"Show me the document"** (for a source-controlled document) is the owner's phrase asking for a **link to the document on GitHub** — not the file's contents inlined, and not a local filesystem path. When the document lives in a GitHub-hosted repo, respond with a GitHub link to it (the file on the relevant branch/commit); only fall back to inlining the contents or giving a local path when the document isn't under source control.
- **Put decisions and approvals in an `AskUserQuestion` popup, not buried in prose.** The owner is frequently on mobile, where a popup notifies and a question embedded in a paragraph is easy to miss. When you need the owner to choose or approve something, ask through the structured popup with explicit options rather than asking in running text. **And when several independent items each need a verdict** (a set of proposed fixes, findings to accept), give **each item its own question carrying its own brief case** — the concrete failure, why it matters, the cost of the fix — with per-item approve/defer options; never one bundled choose-a-scope question over the whole set. The owner decides item-wise, from the case, not from a menu of subsets. (The popup carries at most 4 questions per call — split a larger set into consecutive calls.)
- **When asked to produce a prompt or message destined for another context, visually enclose the whole thing.** Any text the owner will lift out and use elsewhere — a prompt for another assistant, a message to send, copy meant to be pasted somewhere — must be set apart as a single delimited block: either a box (e.g. a fenced code block) or fenced top and bottom by two separating lines, so its exact boundaries are unmistakable and it can be copied in one piece. The point is delimitation, not formatting flair: never leave such content as loose prose the owner has to find the start and end of.
- **Auto-open a PR after committing.** Whenever Claude changes code on a branch and commits it, open a pull request for that branch right after, even without an explicit request to do so. This overrides Claude Code's own default of only opening a PR when explicitly asked.
# Claudinite — active-pack guidance

The baseline plus the packs this project declares. Deeper per-pack reference (e.g. a pack's release doc) is linked from its prose and read on demand.

<!-- pack:basics -->
# Working discipline

The working discipline that isn't itself a GitHub operation — general habits for how to approach a change, independent of any one project.

Start every requested change from the *problem*, not the solution. When the owner asks for a change, don't begin implementing it until the two of you share an explicit understanding of the problem it's meant to solve **and** have agreed that the requested change is the best way to solve it — a different fix, or no change at all, may serve the underlying problem better. Treat the request as a description of a desired outcome, not a specification to execute on sight; surface your read of the problem, raise a better approach if you see one, and reach consensus first. This applies to any repository, not just this one.

An interactive comment from the owner carries one of three build-relevant intents — or none of them — and the classification decides where the change lands and what must exist before any fix. Open your reply to an owner comment with an explicit classification line — `Comment class: correction | feature | process-change | other` (`other` covers questions, approvals, and command phrases; a mixed comment names each part) — the `comment-classification` check enforces that the line was emitted; the assessment itself stays judgment.

1. **A correction** — you misunderstood something. Repair the shared understanding, then rework what the misread already touched — the artifact changes as much as the correction demands; what doesn't appear is a new requirement or rule.
2. **A feature** — a feature run: agree on the requirement, record it in the project's requirements document (its executable spec, where it keeps one), write the test that proves it and watch it fail, then implement until it passes.
3. **A process change** — the owner is changing *how* work is done. The change lands as durable rules in the project's local scope — its own local packs (in Claudinite itself, its packs) — routed through the mechanism promotion ladder (platform setting → hook → check → skill → prose); promoting a rule into the shared canon is the growth lifecycle's separate call, not the interactive session's. Once the intent is understood, author the assurance first: the check the future world must satisfy. Execute it and watch it fail — the failure is the evidence it detects exactly what the owner was worried about — and only then make the fixes that turn it green. When the ladder lands the rule at prose (an in-flight judgment rule no check can carry), the same step is demonstrating the gap: show the corpus doesn't already cover the rule before writing it.

The two build modes share one spine: state the expectation in its durable home first, watch it fail against the current world, then change the world to satisfy it. A fix made before its assurance exists can never show it addressed what the owner actually asked for — and an unrouted comment tends to become a one-off patch where a requirement or a rule should have accumulated.

Confirm a behavior isn't already provided before building a mechanism for it — verify the gap against a real run first; the cheapest fix is often that it already works.

When feedback flags a misunderstanding, don't reflexively expand the artifact to compensate. First check whether it's already correct — if it is, say so and push back rather than editing; a misread doesn't imply the text is wrong (the sibling of the rule above). And size writing to its idea: "open one issue" takes a sentence, not three paragraphs — prose padded to preempt confusion usually breeds it.

When correcting or auditing an artifact against an authoritative source, derive the corrected version from the *source* before reading the existing draft — reading the draft first anchors you to its framing and quietly carries its errors into your "fix." Build from the source, then diff against the old draft to surface what was actually wrong; a clean-room rebuild catches biases an edit-in-place pass silently inherits.

Fix build/test/CI warnings, don't tolerate them: a clean run with no warnings makes a genuinely new warning or error stand out, so noise here costs detection later. Prefer a small, targeted fix that addresses the *cause* in the same change.

Suppressing a warning — muting it with a flag (e.g. `--disable-warning`), `eslint-disable`, swallowing it, etc. — is **not** a small fix: it hides the signal instead of resolving it. Never reach for suppression as the quick path — it's only ever a deliberate, reviewed decision, made once the real fix has been weighed and rejected, never an unattended default. A suppression you do keep must **carry its reason at the site**: an explanation on the suppression line, or in the comment immediately above it, saying why the fix was rejected. That inline reason *is* the review record — it's what separates a considered suppression from a reflexive mute, and it's why the suppression needs no second justification recorded elsewhere.

When a warning can't be fixed with a small cause-addressing change now without hindering current work (e.g. it's waiting on an upstream release, or the real fix is a larger refactor), open a dedicated issue for it (unless one is already open) so it's tracked and not lost — then move on. Resolving it (real fix, or a consciously-chosen suppression) happens in that issue's own change.

An approval — to merge, to ship, to proceed — applies only *backward*, to the work already in front of the owner when it's given, never to anything requested or done *after* it. A later follow-up, even a fix to the just-approved change, needs its own explicit approval; don't carry one approval forward, and don't treat a chosen answer to a multiple-choice prompt as authorization just because an option's wording mentioned the action. When in doubt, surface the new state and wait for a fresh approval.

# The task lifecycle

The issue → branch → PR lifecycle every new task follows, independent of any one project. Its enforcement is the `task-lifecycle` conformance check (it blocks a session from ending with branch commits that reference no issue); this section keeps the method. The rest of the git/GitHub procedures live in the `git-github-advanced` skill.

For every new task:

1. Create a GitHub issue describing the task before starting work.
2. Develop on a branch; reference that issue number in commit messages (e.g. `Refs #123`, `Fixes #123`, or `Closes #123`).
3. Update the issue's status (comments / close) as work progresses and when it's done.

content:
We had a failure during nightly maintenace. Please fix the following:

GoogleCalendarEventCreator baselining hit the gated vendored-mount-flip and found the vendored tree is broken — computeVendorSet omits packs/barriers/ while basics/product-wiki import ../barriers/engine.mjs, so the flipped member would crash. The worker aborted safely and restored GCEC to its coherent pre-flip state. It's a canon-side bug that recurs nightly until fixed.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## github
The GitHub MCP Server provides tools to interact with GitHub platform.

Tool selection guidance:
	1. Use 'list_*' tools for broad, simple retrieval and pagination of all items of a type (e.g., all issues, all PRs, all branches) with basic filtering.
	2. Use 'search_*' tools for targeted queries with specific criteria, keywords, or complex filters (e.g., issues with certain text, PRs by author, code containing functions).

Context management:
	1. Use pagination whenever possible with batches of 5-10 items.
	2. Use minimal_output parameter set to true if the full information is not needed to accomplish a task.

Tool usage guidance:
	1. For 'search_*' tools: Use separate 'sort' and 'order' parameters if available for sorting results - do not include 'sort:' syntax in query strings. Query strings should contain only search criteria (e.g., 'org:google language:python'), not sorting instructions. Always call 'get_me' first to understand current user permissions and context. ## Issues

Check 'list_issue_types' first for organizations to use proper issue types. Use 'search_issues' before creating new issues to avoid duplicates. Always set 'state_reason' when closing issues. ## Pull Requests

PR review workflow: Always use 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'create' to create a pending review, then 'add_comment_to_pending_review' to add comments, and finally 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'submit_pending' to submit the review for complex reviews with line-specific comments.

Before creating a pull request, search for pull request templates in the repository. Template files are called pull_request_template.md or they're located in '.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE' directory. Use the template content to structure the PR description and then call create_pull_request tool.

attachment.content:
- docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- morning: Render the user's morning brief as a styled HTML artifact, or set it up as a recurring weekday task. Use only when the user explicitly asks to run, see, or set up their morning brief, or if they invoke /morning by name. A question about their day, schedule, or calendar is not by itself a request for the brief; answer it directly instead.
- pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- session-start-hook: Creating and developing startup hooks for Claude Code on the web. Use when the user wants to set up a repository for Claude Code on the web, create a SessionStart hook to ensure their project can run tests and linters during web sessions.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- adopt-claudinite: Bootstrap Claudinite into a consuming repo — mount, hooks, checks, skills. Use when asked to bootstrap, adopt, or set up Claudinite, or to baseline a repo to pick up updates.
- authoring-agent-docs: How to write instruction files coding agents follow reliably. Use before writing or editing any Claude instruction doc — a project CLAUDE.md, a convention doc, a routine spec.
- bug-investigation: Method for investigating a bug and pinning down its root cause. Use when investigating a bug report, when a fix didn't hold or a bug recurs, or when a report doesn't reproduce against main.
- bump-version: Raise the project's version — a minor bump by default. Use when the owner says "bump version".
- engineering-practices: General software-engineering practices — naming by scope, single-source-of-truth with drift guards, GENERATED-file discipline, earning each dependency, verifying real platform behavior. Use before writing or editing code.
- file-placement: Where a file should live — the reference-distance metric, the high-reach code smell, and the mandated-location and test-location exemptions. Use before placing, moving, or renaming a file, or when reviewing where one lives.
- generate-project-instructions: Decompose a project into its pack facets (working-style class, technology, aspect, domain) and extract its working instructions into reusable canon packs plus the project's own local packs (.claudinite/local_packs/). Use when a project has no established working style yet, or it exhibits a facet no pack covers — during bootstrap of a fresh/empty repo, or on an uplevel request.
- git-github-advanced: Git/GitHub procedures beyond the baseline lifecycle. Use for commit layering, recovering a branch after a squash-merge, CI-trigger rules, GitHub Actions gotchas, or merge-relocation traps.
- lessons-learned: Retrospective over the current working session, converting its friction into durable improvements. Use when the owner says "learned lessons" / "lessons learned", and after every merge to main (the merge-to-main skill invokes it).
- merge-to-main: Merge the change in front of the owner into main. Use when the owner says "LGTM" or asks to merge/land the current branch or PR into main.
- prose-to-checks: Mine the corpus's existing prose (pack RULES.md, skill SKILL.md) for always-testable rules that were never converted to checks, and convert the strongest ones. Use when auditing the corpus for convertible rules, or when the nightly growth routine runs its prose-to-checks sweep.
- repo-text-sweeps: Mechanics for grep/sed sweeps, renames, and path relocations across a repo. Use before a bulk find-replace, a rename, or moving files — and after one, to catch silently broken references.
- unattended-agents: Architecture and practices for unattended, automation-invoked agents and recurring routines. Use when building, structuring, or running an AI agent, a scheduled routine, or a multi-stage agent pipeline.
- writing-tests: Practices for writing tests you can trust. Use before writing or changing any test — see-it-fail discipline, snapshot/golden rules, CI-only and heavy-browser tests, fuzzy-metric gating.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

message.content:
We had a failure during nightly maintenace. Please fix the following:

GoogleCalendarEventCreator baselining hit the gated vendored-mount-flip and found the vendored tree is broken — computeVendorSet omits packs/barriers/ while basics/product-wiki import ../barriers/engine.mjs, so the flipped member would crash. The worker aborted safely and restored GCEC to its coherent pre-flip state. It's a canon-side bug that recurs nightly until fixed.

content.text: I'll investigate this canon-side bug. Let me start by exploring the relevant code.

content.content:
1	# The vendored mount — persist Claudinite in each consumer, update nightly (design)
2	
3	> **Status: agreed direction (issue #315); phase 0 implemented, phase 1 in progress.** This doc is
4	> the decision record for replacing the session-start fetch with a tracked, nightly-updated
5	> vendor of the corpus. The live mount ([README.md](README.md)) keeps working unchanged until the
6	> transition phases land.
7	
8	## The problem
9	
10	Both mount methods re-fetch the corpus at every session start — Method B pulls the latest-`main`
11	tarball, Method A updates the submodule. That makes session start network-dependent and fragile:
12	the fetch halt-gate, the `codeload.github.com` allowlist requirement, the `.claudinite.new` swap,
13	and the tracked-copy preservation dance in the sync hook all exist only to manage fetch failure.
14	The freshness the fetch buys is not needed: the core rarely changes, packs change roughly
15	nightly, and nothing requires immediate propagation. So the per-session fetch pays a reliability
16	cost every session for a latency guarantee nothing uses.
17	
18	## The model
19	
20	**A consumer holds a vendored, tracked corpus at `.claudinite/shared/`** — ordinary committed
21	files at canon-relative paths, the same move issue #276 already made for the release workflows,
22	applied to the whole corpus. The **nightly maintenance is the only regular writer**.
23	
24	1. **The `shared/` root is a submodule emulation.** The set materializes under
25	   `.claudinite/shared/` mirroring the canon layout exactly, so the planned future — mounting
26	   Claudinite as a **git submodule** at that same path, once sessions run where a cross-repo git
27	   credential exists — is a drop-in upgrade: the submodule lands a superset at the same root and
28	   **no wiring path changes** (`@.claudinite/shared/CLAUDE.md`,
29	   `node .claudinite/shared/checks/stop-hook.mjs`, …). This is also why nothing consumer-owned
30	   lives inside it: `.claudinite/local_packs/` sits *beside* `shared/`, which the submodule
31	   future outright requires — a submodule directory cannot carry the consumer's files.
32	2. **Minimal set, derived structurally from the declaration.** The vendor set is a function of
33	   `.claudinite-checks.json`, computed by [vendor.mjs](vendor.mjs) with **no hand-maintained
34	   file list**: the engine roots (`checks/`, `mount/`) vendored wholesale, the machinery `.mjs`
35	   files at the `packs/`/`skills/` roots, the **declared packs** with their `requires` closure,
36	   the **union of those packs' skills**, and the corpus index (`CLAUDE.md`). Excluded
37	   everywhere: tests; excluded at the engine roots: `*.md` (canon-maintainer docs are read
38	   upstream when needed — a pack's or skill's `.md` files are the payload and ride their
39	   directories). Canon-internal trees (`routines/`, `docs/`, `.github/`, root maintainer docs)
40	   are never vendored. Two accepted edges: a newly declared pack has no local content until the
41	   next refresh (the engine's unknown-pack `config` error surfaces a declared-but-absent pack
42	   loudly), and links from vendored docs to non-vendored canon files may dangle locally by
43	   design (the sweep never inspects the shared mount — see 6).
44	3. **Preferences are never vendored** — per-user settings, not project content. The
45	   session-start step ([inject-preferences.sh](inject-preferences.sh), mount machinery now)
46	   reads the local `preferences/<email>.md` when its tree carries one (the canon repo; a future
47	   submodule mount; the interim full-tarball sync) and otherwise **fetches just that file** over
48	   HTTPS, fresh each session. Every miss — no email, no file, fetch failure — is **fail-soft**:
49	   a one-line note and the session proceeds on defaults. The halt-gate is reserved for the
50	   load-bearing corpus, which after the flip is always local and can't miss.
51	4. **Transactional nightly update.** Per repo and per night: apply the pending migration notes,
52	   converge the vendored tree to the canon snapshot, advance the stamp — **one commit**. (One
53	   MCP reality: file *deletions* can't ride a `push_files` commit, so convergence's prunes
54	   follow as their own `delete_file` commits **after** the stamped content commit — safe to
55	   interrupt, since the next night's convergence re-deletes stragglers; note ops are
56	   stamp-gated and always land with the stamp — #329.) If the
57	   migration fails, nothing is written: the repo keeps running its old snapshot exactly as
58	   before, is retried the next night, and the failure lands in the fleet routine's failure log.
59	   The commit honors the repo's `maintenance.delivery` (`push` or `pr`; the pr lane computes its
60	   writes against the *default* branch, drops what the maintenance branch already carries, and
61	   refreshes that branch from base each night — regenerate, never reconcile — #332). The refresh is
62	   **unconditional convergence** (copy-if-different, not copy-if-canon-moved), so an accidental
63	   local edit to a vendored file reverts within a day, visibly in the nightly's diff — that,
64	   plus a one-line "canon-owned; propose changes upstream" note, is the whole anti-drift story;
65	   there is deliberately no manifest/integrity framework. Direction-blindness cuts both ways,
66	   so the writer carries the one **anti-rewind guard** the no-framework stance leaves room for:
67	   converging is refused when the checkout's HEAD mismatches the passed ref or the member's
68	   stamped ref is not its ancestor, and the worker verifies the checkout is at the **remote**
69	   head before converging — a stale maintenance checkout must fail its unit, never silently
70	   downgrade the fleet (#328).
71	5. **The stamp.** `"claudinite": { "updated": "<full ISO datetime>", "ref": "<sha>" }` in
72	   `.claudinite-checks.json` — `updated` selects which migration notes still apply (notes are
73	   day-dated, so selection is by the stamp's **day, same-day inclusive** — a note landing later
74	   on the stamp's day must still apply, and note idempotency absorbs the re-application this
75	   admits — #330), `ref` is provenance for debugging **and** the anti-rewind guard's anchor (4). **One stamp for the whole set, never per pack**: updates are
76	   whole-set atomic, because mixed per-pack versions inside one repo would recreate exactly the
77	   engine↔pack skew this design eliminates (declaring a new pack therefore triggers a whole-set
78	   refresh, not a lone pack copy). The engine already tolerates the key
79	   (`CONFIG_KEYS` in checks/lib).
80	6. **The shared mount is structurally out of the sweep.** The engine's file-set builder drops
81	   everything under `.claudinite/shared/` (`buildContext` in checks/lib) — the corpus is
82	   canon-owned, never the project's own code — while `.claudinite/local_packs/` stays fully in
83	   scope. Deliberately a structural rule in the engine, **not** a `.gitattributes` /
84	   `linguist-vendored` convention: the exclusion must hold on any git host and any checkout.
85	   This is also what keeps the corpus-shape checks (catalog-completeness, skill-ownership)
86	   naturally inert in consumers with a pruned tree.
87	7. **Pinning semantics.** Each branch runs the snapshot committed on it: a session, its Stop
88	   hook, and CI all judge by the same law, and a canon change affects no consumer until that
89	   consumer's own nightly commit. Consumer CI becomes a three-line workflow running
90	   `node .claudinite/shared/checks/run.mjs` from the checkout — the backstop
91	   [checks/README.md](../checks/README.md) names finally has standard wiring. Rollback is
92	   per-repo and atomic: revert the nightly's commit.
93	8. **Isolation.** Consumer files must not reference `.claudinite/` except the wiring set: the
94	   root `CLAUDE.md` (the `@`-import and self-check), `.claude/` (settings hook registrations),
95	   `.gitignore`, `.gitattributes`, `.github/workflows/` (the CI stub), and anything under
96	   `.claudinite/` itself (`local_packs/` included). Product code that wants a canon helper
97	   inlines it — depending on canon internals would turn every canon refactor into a breaking
98	   migration for code the canon doesn't own. Enforced as a **basics check composed on the
99	   barriers pack's exported detection engine** — the composition pattern packs already use with
100	   each other — so it is universal via basics, with no per-project barriers declaration or
101	   config to maintain; it self-gates on `.claudinite/` existing, so it is inert in the canon
102	   repo.
103	9. **Migration notes v2.** A canon change that consumers must be amended for ships as a dated
104	   record (the existing `migrations/active_migrations/` shape): mechanical ops where code can
105	   express them, plus a **brief agentic note** for what it can't (chiefly adapting
106	   consumer-authored `local_packs/` content to a changed engine contract). The nightly applies
107	   the notes dated on or after the day of the repo's stamp (same-day inclusive, note
108	   idempotency absorbing the overlap — #330), oldest first, inside the one transactional commit.
109	   No read-side tolerances in live code, no `LEGACY_*` constants, and **no per-consumer state
110	   held in the canon** — the stamp in each consumer is the only bookkeeping. **Retirement is a
111	   retention window**: a note is deleted ~5 weeks after landing; a repo lagging longer has been
112	   failing loudly in the nightly log (or was off the access list), and its catch-up path is
113	   re-running adoption, which vendors head idempotently. The one surviving two-phase case is
114	   **out-of-repo state** no commit can reach — the pasted web-environment Setup script keeps
115	   the probe + halt-gate pattern ([consumer-safe-changes.md](../consumer-safe-changes.md)).
116	10. **Accepted trade-off.** A bad canon change (typically an overzealous blocking check)
117	    reaches consumers on one nightly and its fix on a later one — up to ~two days' exposure.
118	    Dampers: the Stop hook's own two-block self-release, per-repo `rules`/`accept` overrides,
119	    and an on-demand refresh (the nightly's update step run in-session) as the
120	    pull-forward/emergency lane.
121	
122	**What this retires:** the per-session fetch and its halt-gate, the `codeload.github.com`
123	allowlist prerequisite, `CLAUDINITE_REF` pinning (the commit *is* the pin), the
124	`.claudinite.new` swap and tracked-copy preservation, the Method A/B split with the submodule
125	caveats, and the stale-mount caveat in [checks/README.md](../checks/README.md). Session start
126	becomes a single offline SessionStart entry invoking [session-start.sh](session-start.sh)
127	directly (its four steps are unchanged; the preferences step is fail-soft per 3, the env-check
128	halt-gate stays); the `CLAUDE.md` self-check line remains the absent-corpus tell. The
129	plugin-packaging rationale in [checks/DESIGN.md](../checks/DESIGN.md) also loses its
130	update-latency premise — recorded there when the transition completes. The canon repo itself is
131	untouched: it runs its own live tree and mounts nothing.
132	
133	## Applying it to the fleet — the transition
134	
135	Per [consumer-safe-changes.md](../consumer-safe-changes.md): pilot on one real consumer before
136	the nightly touches everyone, and never break the channel the migration itself travels through.
137	
138	- **Phase 0 (done):** this record + [vendor.mjs](vendor.mjs), the vendor-set computation
139	  everything else builds on.
140	- **Phase 1 — canon capabilities:** the fail-soft preferences step (done); the `claudinite`
141	  stamp key in `loadConfig` (done); the engine's shared-mount sweep exclusion (done); the local
142	  vendor writer [apply-vendor.mjs](apply-vendor.mjs) — whole-set convergence + stamp, erroring
143	  before any write (done); the consumer CI stub, shipped in the baseline pack's `stubs/` (done);
144	  the isolation check (done — the reference index builds from `ctx.tracked`, so vendored files
145	  stay resolvable as reference *targets* while excluded from scanning); the baselining worker
146	  branches on the `claudinite` stamp — vendored members get the transactional refresh
147	  (notes → converge → stamp, one commit), pre-flip members get legacy maintenance only, never an
148	  ungated flip (done); [bootstrap.md](../bootstrap.md) rewritten around the vendored fresh path,
149	  with the legacy shapes quarantined in a **retiring transition appendix** the worker uses on
150	  unflipped members (done — the appendix, not dated notes, carries the transition-window
151	  maintenance; the flip note carries the conversion); fleet membership discovery reduces to the
152	  **single** probe every member carries whatever its mount shape — the tracked
153	  `.claudinite-checks.json`, the only shape the planner can plan for at all; a mount marker
154	  without a declaration classifies uncovered and heals through an adoption issue (done).
155	- **Phase 2 — the flip (shipped, pilot-gated):** the dated note
156	  (`migrations/active_migrations/`, `vendored-mount-flip`) converts a member in one commit —
157	  vendor set under `.claudinite/shared/`, the `.gitignore` flip (keep ignoring `.claudinite/*`
158	  with `shared/` and `local_packs/` re-included, so legacy flat leftovers and a stale
159	  environment's stray sync stay invisible), the `SessionStart`/Stop/PreToolUse rewrite to
160	  `shared/` paths, the `CLAUDE.md` import + self-check swap, the CI stub, the stamp, and the
161	  sync-hook deletion — executed by the **baselining worker** (the mechanical passes see the
162	  record as a no-op; its `legacyPresent` feeds the unflipped-count telemetry), plus one
163	  member issue asking to re-paste the environment Setup script (the surviving out-of-repo
164	  action). **Gated pilot: the note names only `GoogleCalendarEventCreator`**; after a clean
165	  night, widening to the fleet is a one-line edit (`flip.repos: 'fleet'`). Fleet discovery is untouched by the flip —
166	  it keys on the tracked `.claudinite-checks.json`, which both mount shapes carry (phase 1) —
167	  so no member can be orphaned by its migration state. A session already running when the flip lands
168	  keeps its old wiring against its old snapshot — coherent — and picks the new world up next
169	  session.
170	- **Phase 3 — converge and retire:** once every member is flipped,
171	  `sync-claudinite.sh` is deleted from the canon, bootstrap's transition appendix
172	  is pruned, and [consumer-safe-changes.md](../consumer-safe-changes.md) is rewritten to
173	  the new, much smaller channel model (tracked-vendor commit + notes; the instant `@main` and
174	  session-sync channels are gone).
175	
176	## The vendor-set contract ([vendor.mjs](vendor.mjs))
177	
178	`computeVendorSet(declaredEntries, { extraSkills })` → `{ files, errors }`, computed against the
179	canon tree the module ships in; the writer materializes `files` under `SHARED_SUBDIR`
180	(exported; defined beside `LOCAL_PACKS_SUBDIR` in the packs registry). Structural throughout:
181	`ENGINE_DIR_ROOTS` walks (tests and root-docs excluded), `MACHINERY_ROOTS` top-level `.mjs`,
182	resolved canon packs' directories, the skills union — never a per-file list. Declared ids that
183	name no canon pack (a consumer's local packs, or a typo the runner's settings validation
184	already flags) are skipped without error; a pack-required skill missing from the tree is
185	reported in `errors`. `extraSkills` lets a caller add skills the canon can't see — e.g. ones
186	required by a member's own local packs.
187

# The vendored mount — persist Claudinite in each consumer, update nightly (design)

> **Status: agreed direction (issue #315); phase 0 implemented, phase 1 in progress.** This doc is
> the decision record for replacing the session-start fetch with a tracked, nightly-updated
> vendor of the corpus. The live mount ([README.md](README.md)) keeps working unchanged until the
> transition phases land.

## The problem

tarball, Method A updates the submodule. That makes session start network-dependent and fragile:
the fetch halt-gate, the `codeload.github.com` allowlist requirement, the `.claudinite.new` swap,
and the tracked-copy preservation dance in the sync hook all exist only to manage fetch failure.
The freshness the fetch buys is not needed: the core rarely changes, packs change roughly
nightly, and nothing requires immediate propagation. So the per-session fetch pays a reliability
cost every session for a latency guarantee nothing uses.

## The model

**A consumer holds a vendored, tracked corpus at `.claudinite/shared/`** — ordinary committed

applied to the whole corpus. The **nightly maintenance is the only regular writer**.

   `.claudinite/shared/` mirroring the canon layout exactly, so the planned future — mounting

   future outright requires — a submodule directory cannot carry the consumer's files.
2. **Minimal set, derived structurally from the declaration.** The vendor set is a function of
   `.claudinite-checks.json`, computed by [vendor.mjs](vendor.mjs) with **no hand-maintained
   file list**: the engine roots (`checks/`, `mount/`) vendored wholesale, the machinery `.mjs`

   are never vendored. Two accepted edges: a newly declared pack has no local content until the
   next refresh (the engine's unknown-pack `config` error surfaces a declared-but-absent pack

3. **Preferences are never vendored** — per-user settings, not project content. The

   a one-line note and the session proceeds on defaults. The halt-gate is reserved for the

   follow as their own `delete_file` commits **after** the stamped content commit — safe to

   stamp-gated and always land with the stamp — #329.) If the
   migration fails, nothing is written: the repo keeps running its old snapshot exactly as

   each other — so it is universal via basics, with no per-project barriers declaration or
   config to maintain; it self-gates on `.claudinite/` existing, so it is inert in the canon
   repo.
9. **Migration notes v2.** A canon change that consumers must be amended for ships as a dated
   record (the existing `migrations/active_migrations/` shape): mechanical ops where code can
   express them, plus a **brief agentic note** for what it can't (chiefly adapting
   consumer-authored `local_packs/` content to a changed engine contract). The nightly applies
   the notes dated on or after the day of the repo's stamp (same-day inclusive, note
   idempotency absorbing the overlap — #330), oldest first, inside the one transactional commit.
   No read-side tolerances in live code, no `LEGACY_*` constants, and **no per-consumer state
   held in the canon** — the stamp in each consumer is the only bookkeeping. **Retirement is a
   retention window**: a note is deleted ~5 weeks after landing; a repo lagging longer has been
   failing loudly in the nightly log (or was off the access list), and its catch-up path is
   re-running adoption, which vendors head idempotently. The one surviving two-phase case is
   **out-of-repo state** no commit can reach — the pasted web-environment Setup script keeps

    reaches consumers on one nightly and its fix on a later one — up to ~two days' exposure.

## Applying it to the fleet — the transition

Per [consumer-safe-changes.md](../consumer-safe-changes.md): pilot on one real consumer before
the nightly touches everyone, and never break the channel the migration itself travels through.

- **Phase 0 (done):** this record + [vendor.mjs](vendor.mjs), the vendor-set computation
  everything else builds on.
- **Phase 1 — canon capabilities:** the fail-soft preferences step (done); the `claudinite`
  stamp key in `loadConfig` (done); the engine's shared-mount sweep exclusion (done); the local
  vendor writer [apply-vendor.mjs](apply-vendor.mjs) — whole-set convergence + stamp, erroring
  before any write (done); the consumer CI stub, shipped in the baseline pack's `stubs/` (done);
  the isolation check (done — the reference index builds from `ctx.tracked`, so vendored files
  stay resolvable as reference *targets* while excluded from scanning); the baselining worker
  branches on the `claudinite` stamp — vendored members get the transactional refresh
  (notes → converge → stamp, one commit), pre-flip members get legacy maintenance only, never an
  ungated flip (done); [bootstrap.md](../bootstrap.md) rewritten around the vendored fresh path,
  with the legacy shapes quarantined in a **retiring transition appendix** the worker uses on
  unflipped members (done — the appendix, not dated notes, carries the transition-window
  maintenance; the flip note carries the conversion); fleet membership discovery reduces to the
  **single** probe every member carries whatever its mount shape — the tracked
  `.claudinite-checks.json`, the only shape the planner can plan for at all; a mount marker
  without a declaration classifies uncovered and heals through an adoption issue (done).
- **Phase 2 — the flip (shipped, pilot-gated):** the dated note
  (`migrations/active_migrations/`, `vendored-mount-flip`) converts a member in one commit —
  vendor set under `.claudinite/shared/`, the `.gitignore` flip (keep ignoring `.claudinite/*`
  with `shared/` and `local_packs/` re-included, so legacy flat leftovers and a stale
  environment's stray sync stay invisible), the `SessionStart`/Stop/PreToolUse rewrite to
  `shared/` paths, the `CLAUDE.md` import + self-check swap, the CI stub, the stamp, and the
  sync-hook deletion — executed by the **baselining worker** (the mechanical passes see the
  record as a no-op; its `legacyPresent` feeds the unflipped-count telemetry), plus one
  member issue asking to re-paste the environment Setup script (the surviving out-of-repo
  action). **Gated pilot: the note names only `GoogleCalendarEventCreator`**; after a clean
  night, widening to the fleet is a one-line edit (`flip.repos: 'fleet'`). Fleet discovery is untouched by the flip —
  it keys on the tracked `.claudinite-checks.json`, which both mount shapes carry (phase 1) —
  so no member can be orphaned by its migration state. A session already running when the flip lands
  keeps its old wiring against its old snapshot — coherent — and picks the new world up next
  session.
- **Phase 3 — converge and retire:** once every member is flipped,
  `sync-claudinite.sh` is deleted from the canon, bootstrap's transition appendix
  is pruned, and [consumer-safe-changes.md](../consumer-safe-changes.md) is rewritten to
  the new, much smaller channel model (tracked-vendor commit + notes; the instant `@main` and
  session-sync channels are gone).

## The vendor-set contract ([vendor.mjs](vendor.mjs))

`computeVendorSet(declaredEntries, { extraSkills })` → `{ files, errors }`, computed against the
canon tree the module ships in; the writer materializes `files` under `SHARED_SUBDIR`
(exported; defined beside `LOCAL_PACKS_SUBDIR` in the packs registry). Structural throughout:
`ENGINE_DIR_ROOTS` walks (tests and root-docs excluded), `MACHINERY_ROOTS` top-level `.mjs`,
resolved canon packs' directories, the skills union — never a per-file list. Declared ids that
name no canon pack (a consumer's local packs, or a typo the runner's settings validation
already flags) are skipped without error; a pack-required skill missing from the tree is
reported in `errors`. `extraSkills` lets a caller add skills the canon can't see — e.g. ones
required by a member's own local packs.

lines: +// Static-import closure over the discovered set. Structural discovery reaches a
+// file only through its pack/skill/engine home, but a vendored .mjs can import a
+// canon file that lives *outside* that home — chiefly a pack composing another
+// pack's exported engine (basics and product-wiki build their isolation barrier
+// on packs/barriers/engine.mjs without declaring barriers, by design — mount
+// DESIGN.md §8), or a check reading migrations/registry.mjs. Those are real
+// module edges the flipped member loads at runtime, so a coherent set must carry
+// every file transitively reachable by import from the files already in it.
+// Without this, the vendored member crashes on the first missing import — the
+// gated flip's whole reason to abort. Purely additive: it only pulls in more
+// canon files (and reports an import that resolves to nothing, i.e. canon-side
+// breakage, so convergence aborts rather than shipping a tree that won't load).
+// Matches `from '<spec>'`, side-effect `import '<spec>'`, and dynamic
+// `import('<spec>')` for relative specifiers only (a bare specifier is a node
+// builtin or a dependency, neither vendored). No .mjs in the corpus carries a
+// relative specifier inside a comment, so scanning raw source is safe.
+const RELATIVE_SPEC = /(?:\bfrom|\bimport)\s*\(?\s*['"](\.[^'"]+)['"]/g;
+function resolveImport(fromRel, spec) {
+  const base = posix.normalize(posix.join(posix.dirname(fromRel), spec));
+  const candidates = base.endsWith('.mjs') || base.endsWith('.js')
+    ? [base]
+    : [base, `${base}.mjs`, `${base}.js`, `${base}/index.mjs`];
+  return candidates.find((c) => existsSync(join(canonRoot, c))) ?? null;
+function closeOverImports(files, errors) {
+  const queue = [...files].filter((f) => f.endsWith('.mjs'));
+  while (queue.length) {
+    const rel = queue.shift();
+    let src;
+    try {
+      src = readFileSync(join(canonRoot, rel), 'utf8');
+    } catch { continue; }
+    for (const [, spec] of src.matchAll(RELATIVE_SPEC)) {
+      const resolved = resolveImport(rel, spec);
+      if (!resolved) {
+        errors.push({
+          what: `${rel} imports "${spec}", which resolves to no file in the canon tree`,
+          fix: 'fix the import specifier, or restore the file it names',
+        continue;
+      if (!files.has(resolved)) {
+        files.add(resolved);
+        if (resolved.endsWith('.mjs')) queue.push(resolved);

assert.ok(!files.includes('skills/s1/helper.test.mjs'));
+// The import closure — a declared pack composing an UNDECLARED pack's exported
+// engine (the barriers pattern basics/product-wiki use) must still vendor the
+// imported file, transitively, or the flipped member crashes on the missing
+// import. Only the imported files ride along, never the whole undeclared pack.
+test('import closure pulls a composed sibling pack\'s engine (and its transitive imports) into the set', async () => {
+  const root = makeCanon({
+    packs: [
+      { id: 'consumer', extraFiles: [{ file: 'check.mjs', content: "import { under } from '../engine-pack/engine.mjs';\nexport default under;\n" }] },
+      { id: 'engine-pack', extraFiles: [
+        { file: 'engine.mjs', content: "import { helper } from './support.mjs';\nexport const under = helper;\n" },
+        { file: 'support.mjs', content: 'export const helper = 1;\n' },
+  const { files, errors } = await vendorAt(root, ['consumer']);
+  assert.deepEqual(errors, []);
+  assert.ok(files.includes('packs/consumer/check.mjs'));
+  assert.ok(files.includes('packs/engine-pack/engine.mjs'), 'the imported engine must vendor');
+  assert.ok(files.includes('packs/engine-pack/support.mjs'), 'its transitive import must vendor too');
+  // engine-pack is not declared: only the imported files ride along, not its pack.mjs
+  assert.ok(!files.includes('packs/engine-pack/pack.mjs'), 'the undeclared pack itself does not vendor wholesale');
+test('import closure resolves engine-root imports without duplicating an already-present file', async () => {
+  const root = makeCanon({
+    packs: [{ id: 'consumer', extraFiles: [{ file: 'check.mjs', content: "import { finding } from '../../checks/lib/context.mjs';\nexport default finding;\n" }] }],
+  const { files, errors } = await vendorAt(root, ['consumer']);
+  assert.deepEqual(errors, []);
+  assert.equal(files.filter((f) => f === 'checks/lib/context.mjs').length, 1);
+test('an import resolving to no canon file is a hard error (convergence must abort, not ship a broken tree)', async () => {
+  const root = makeCanon({
+    packs: [{ id: 'consumer', extraFiles: [{ file: 'check.mjs', content: "import x from '../ghost/missing.mjs';\nexport default x;\n" }] }],
+  const { errors } = await vendorAt(root, ['consumer']);
+  assert.equal(errors.length, 1);
+  assert.match(errors[0].what, /missing\.mjs/);
+  assert.match(errors[0].what, /packs\/consumer\/check\.mjs/);
+// Regression against the real corpus for the reported nightly failure: basics
+// and product-wiki compose packs/barriers/engine.mjs but do not declare barriers,
+// so the structural discovery alone omitted it and the flipped member crashed.
+test('real corpus: composing packs vendor packs/barriers/engine.mjs even without declaring barriers', async () => {
+  const { computeVendorSet } = await import('./vendor.mjs');
+  for (const pack of ['basics', 'product-wiki']) {
+    const { files, errors } = await computeVendorSet([pack]);
+    assert.deepEqual(errors, [], `${pack}: vendor set must have no import-resolution errors`);
+    assert.ok(files.includes('packs/barriers/engine.mjs'), `${pack} composes barriers/engine.mjs — it must vendor`);

input.title: vendor: close the vendor set over static imports so composed engines ride along
input.head: claude/gcec-vendored-tree-fix-z6fuef
input.body:
## What broke

Nightly baselining of `GoogleCalendarEventCreator` hit the gated vendored-mount flip and found the vendored tree would be incoherent: `computeVendorSet` omits `packs/barriers/` while `basics`/`product-wiki` import `../barriers/engine.mjs`, so the flipped member would crash on the missing import. The worker aborted safely and restored GCEC to its pre-flip state — but it's a canon-side bug that recurs every night until fixed.

## Root cause

`computeVendorSet` reached a file only through its structural home (engine roots wholesale, machinery `.mjs` at the roots, declared packs' directories, the skills union). But a vendored `.mjs` can import a canon file that lives *outside* that home. The design deliberately has `basics` and `product-wiki` build their isolation barrier on `packs/barriers/engine.mjs` **without** declaring the `barriers` pack (the declaration-free composition pattern, mount `DESIGN.md` §8) — so the composed engine was never discovered, and nothing pulled it in.

## Fix

Add a **static-import closure** to `computeVendorSet`: after structural discovery, walk the relative-import graph to a fixpoint, adding every canon file transitively reachable by import from the files already in the set. It is:

- **Structural, not a hand-maintained list** — it follows real module edges (`from '…'`, side-effect `import '…'`, dynamic `import('…')`), matching the design's "no per-file list" ethos.
- **Purely additive** — it only pulls in more canon files. An import that resolves to *no* canon file is surfaced as an error, so convergence aborts (the existing errors-before-any-write contract) rather than shipping a tree that won't load.

This also closes a latent instance of the same class: `chrome-extension-release` statically imports `../../migrations/registry.mjs`, which was likewise never vendored. Audited across every declarable pack: **zero dangling static imports remain**.

## Tests

- Import closure pulls a composed sibling pack's engine (and its transitive imports) into the set, without vendoring the undeclared pack wholesale.
- An engine-root import already present is not duplicated.
- An import resolving to no canon file is a hard error.
- Real-corpus regression: declaring `basics` or `product-wiki` now vendors `packs/barriers/engine.mjs` with no errors — the exact case that failed nightly.

Full corpus suite (349 tests) and `checks/run.mjs` both green.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

https://claude.ai/code/session_01DJX6fa8A293pv5jK4ucAXy

**Fix** (`mount/vendor.mjs`): a static-import closure that, after structural discovery, walks the relative-import graph to a fixpoint and pulls in every transitively-reachable canon file. It's structural (follows real `from`/`import`/dynamic-`import` edges, no hand-maintained list), purely additive, and reports an import that resolves to nothing as an error so convergence aborts rather than shipping a broken tree.

It also closed a **latent second instance** of the same class — `chrome-extension-release` → `../../migrations/registry.mjs` — which was never vendored either. I audited across all declarable packs: **zero dangling static imports remain**.

But a vendored `.mjs` can statically import a canon file *outside* that home. content: So, you're saying that one pack is using the code from another pack, and the solution is some convoluted way to find the files? No, the solution is to: 1) prevent packs from referring to each other's code, and 2) use the second pack's abilities by adding configuration that uses it. Any time a rule states "X needs to happen before Y" there should be a check-the-work test that verifies that either on the conversation here, or on the branch commit log.

1	import { existsSync } from 'node:fs';
2	import { join } from 'node:path';
3	import { defineBarrier } from '../barriers/engine.mjs';
4	import { SHARED_SUBDIR } from '../registry.mjs';
5	
6	// Consumers must not couple to the vendored canon (mount/DESIGN.md): outside
7	// the wiring set, no file may reference .claudinite/ — product code that wants
8	// a canon helper inlines it, because depending on canon internals turns every
9	// canon refactor into a breaking migration for code the canon doesn't own.
10	// Composed on the barriers pack's exported engine (the fixed-graph pattern
11	// product-wiki established); gated on the vendored mount existing
12	// (.claudinite/shared/), so it is inert in the canon repo and in pre-flip
13	// consumers.
14	//
15	// The wiring carve-outs — the files that legitimately spell .claudinite/ paths:
16	// - '.claudinite': the mount subtree itself (local_packs/ referencing the canon;
17	//   the vendored files are already outside the scanned set).
18	// - '.claude': settings.json registers the hooks by path.
19	// - '.github/workflows': the CI stub runs the vendored engine.
20	// - 'CLAUDE.md': the @-import and the self-check line.
21	// - '.gitignore', '.gitattributes': the mount's housekeeping rules.
22	// - '.claudinite-checks.json': settings legitimately spell paths.
23	// Coverage note: quoted references (imports, requires, config values) and
24	// relative traversals resolve and fire; a bare unquoted `.claudinite/...` in
25	// prose falls outside the engine's candidate shapes — accepted, since
26	// imports/wiring are the coupling class that matters.
27	const barrier = defineBarrier({
28	  id: 'claudinite-isolation',
29	  description: 'Outside the wiring files (CLAUDE.md, .claude/, .gitignore, .gitattributes, .github/workflows/, the settings file), nothing may reference .claudinite/ — inline what you need instead of importing the canon',
30	  why: 'the vendored canon is refreshed nightly and refactored upstream; code that reaches into it inherits every canon rename as a breaking change',
31	  doc: 'mount/DESIGN.md',
32	  crossingExcuse: 'if the crossing is deliberate, excuse it with accept: [{ "rule": "claudinite-isolation", "path": "<file>", "reason": "..." }] in .claudinite-checks.json (a pack-shipped barrier takes no per-rule except entries)',
33	  edges: [{
34	    from: '.',
35	    to: '.claudinite',
36	    matchUniqueFilenames: false,
37	    except: ['.claudinite', '.claude', '.github/workflows', 'CLAUDE.md', '.gitignore', '.gitattributes', '.claudinite-checks.json'],
38	    reason: 'consumer files must not couple to the vendored canon — the wiring files are the reviewed exceptions (mount/DESIGN.md)',
39	  }],
40	});
41	
42	export default {
43	  ...barrier,
44	  run(ctx) {
45	    if (!existsSync(join(ctx.root, SHARED_SUBDIR))) return [];
46	    return barrier.run(ctx);
47	  },
48	};
49

1	import { defineBarrier } from '../barriers/engine.mjs';
2	import { PRODUCT_ROOT, SINK_DIR } from './lib.mjs';
3	
4	// The wall: nothing outside product-wiki/ may reference the self-growing wiki
5	// folders; product-wiki/product-requirements is the one reviewed crossing point.
6	// This edge and product-wiki-layout are a designed pair — the glob target
7	// fails closed ("matched no directories", blocking) on an empty product-wiki/
8	// expansion, and layout owns the missing-skeleton complaint. Under the
9	// structural standard a renamed wiki folder is still a wiki folder, still
10	// barred — no per-folder disarm hole.
11	//
12	// Edge notes (each empirically verified against a real consumer tree):
13	// - to 'product-wiki/*' bars every direct CHILD DIRECTORY of product-wiki/; files
14	//   directly under product-wiki/ (the index README) stay reachable — a repo's root
15	//   CLAUDE.md legitimately links product-wiki/README.md.
16	// - allow keeps the crossing point reachable from every guarded file.
17	// - except 'product-wiki' unguards the whole product-wiki/ subtree (wikis reference
18	//   each other, sample-data, and outward freely — the wall is one-directional)
19	//   and satisfies the root-guard validation for the glob target.
20	// - except '.claudinite-checks.json': the settings file legitimately spells
21	//   wiki paths (accept entries, historical config) — configuration is not a
22	//   dependency.
23	// - matchUniqueFilenames OFF: wiki/sample-data filenames are agent-written,
24	//   so leaving the unique-basename layer on would let every distinctive
25	//   filename the growth routine invents become a repo-wide barred bare name
26	//   outside the consumer's review. Path references still fire.
27	// - No baked reviewed-exceptions, and consumers can't add any to a
28	//   pack-shipped edge — crossingExcuse points each finding at the lever that
29	//   works (an accept; see packs/product-wiki/README.md). Unlike a rule-owned
30	//   except, accepts are not staleness-audited — prune them by hand.
31	export default defineBarrier({
32	  id: 'product-wiki-isolation',
33	  description: 'Nothing outside product-wiki/ may reference the self-growing wiki folders — product-wiki/product-requirements is the only crossing point',
34	  why: 'the wikis are agent-rewritten, loosely-sourced research — code, tests, and docs that silently depend on them inherit unreviewed churn',
35	  doc: 'packs/product-wiki/README.md',
36	  crossingExcuse: 'if the crossing is deliberate, excuse it with accept: [{ "rule": "product-wiki-isolation", "path": "<file>", "reason": "..." }] in .claudinite-checks.json (a pack-shipped barrier takes no per-rule except entries — see packs/product-wiki/README.md)',
37	  edges: [{
38	    from: '.',
39	    to: `${PRODUCT_ROOT}/*`,
40	    allow: [SINK_DIR],
41	    matchUniqueFilenames: false,
42	    except: [PRODUCT_ROOT, '.claudinite-checks.json'],
43	    reason: 'the self-growing product wikis and their sample data are autonomous research the repo must not depend on; product-wiki/product-requirements is the one reviewed crossing point',
44	  }],
45	});
46
