1	// Enable a repo's own local packs (.claudinite/local_packs/ — tracked project
2	// prose/checks/skills/run_daily the engine runs alongside the canon). Two pieces
3	// of member-side plumbing must be in place before a repo can safely COMMIT local
4	// packs, and BOTH ride baselining (not this record):
5	//   1. the tracked sync hook must preserve local_packs/ across its dir swap —
6	//      baselining refreshes the member's tracked mount/sync-claudinite.sh from the
7	//      canon every run (the canon copy carries the preserve block); and
8	//   2. .gitignore must re-include .claudinite/local_packs/ — bootstrap.md step 3
9	//      (idempotent, re-applied by baselining) appends the negation.
10	// The canon commit that ships those touches mount/ and bootstrap.md, so
11	// canonChanged fires fleet-wide and baselining converges every member the first
12	// night after — no per-repo content is materialized here (a project AUTHORS its
13	// own local packs; the move of existing capture is a judgment task carried by the
14	// project-instructions and growth-extract skills, never a mechanical alias).
15	//
16	// This record is therefore TELEMETRY ONLY: the retire pass reports how many
17	// members still carry the pre-enablement hook, so the owner can see when the whole
18	// fleet is safe to author local packs in. `legacyPresent` reads the member's
19	// tracked hook and returns true while it lacks the local_packs preserve block.
20	//
21	// retire: 'manual' — the enablement lives in baselining + bootstrap, not here, so
22	// this record holds no tolerance to strand; retire it by hand once the retire pass
23	// reports zero members on the legacy shape.
24	export default {
25	  id: 'local-packs-enablement',
26	  landed: '2026-07-15',
27	  summary: 'members preserve + track .claudinite/local_packs/ (sync-hook preserve via baselining, gitignore via bootstrap)',
28	  legacyPresent: async (exists, read) => {
29	    const hook = await read('.claudinite/mount/sync-claudinite.sh');
30	    if (hook == null) return false; // pre-mount shape — the mount-folder migration tracks that, not this
31	    // Converged once the tracked hook carries the preserve block for local_packs.
32	    return !hook.includes('$dest/local_packs');
33	  },
34	  retire: 'manual',
35	};

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Extending Claudinite — the core/pack boundary and where a feature goes

Before adding *anything* to this repo, decide which of two layers it belongs to. Getting this
wrong is the recurring failure mode — a rule, a technology's conventions, or a nightly task
wired straight into the engine instead of contributed as pack content, where every consumer then
pays for it whether or not they need it.

## The one principle

> **Core is the engine that runs pack-contributed content. A feature is core only if it *is* the
> engine.** Everything a project could plausibly not need — a rule, a technology's gotchas, a
> maintenance task, a project-class playbook — is pack content, discovered structurally and
> activated by declaration. If you can't say "this is machinery every pack relies on," it isn't
> core.

This is the same instinct the corpus already applies to itself: packs and skills are discovered
by *scanning the tree*, not by a hand-maintained list ([packs/registry.mjs](packs/registry.mjs),
[skills/registry.mjs](skills/registry.mjs)); the mechanism is fixed, the content is open. New
content drops in a directory; new mechanism is a deliberate change to the engine.

## What is core (the engine)

Core is the small, closed set of machinery that runs, discovers, and schedules content. Touch it
only to extend the *mechanism*, never to add one project's rule or task:

| Engine piece | Home | What it is |
|---|---|---|
| Checks runner + hooks | [`checks/`](checks/README.md) | the dependency-free runner, its lib, the Stop hook, the PreToolUse guard — runs the packs' checks; owns no rule itself |
| Pack discovery + prose injection | `packs/registry.mjs`, `packs/load-active-prose.mjs` | structural scan of `packs/*/pack.mjs`; SessionStart injection of active packs' prose |
| Skill discovery + mounting | `skills/registry.mjs`, `skills/mount-skills.mjs` | structural scan; per-session symlink of the active packs' skill union |
| Adoption interviews | `packs/interview.mjs` | the gap computation (a pack's declared questions minus the entry's stored answers) and the SessionStart nudge; owns no question itself |
| Baseline-migration mechanism | [`migrations/`](migrations/README.md) | the read-side resolver, write-side rename, and fleet telemetry that auto-retires a relocation once every consumer has moved |
| The run_daily planner | [`routines/fleet/`](routines/fleet/DESIGN.md) | goes over the reachable repos, assembles each one's due tasks from its active packs, masks full-sweep, isolates a throwing gate, emits the plan; pack-agnostic, owns no task, depends on no pack |
| The orchestrator | [`routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md`](routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md) | the single scheduled entry point — runs the planner over the accessible fleet, reads the plan, fans out the units |
| Bootstrap / baselining | [`bootstrap.md`](bootstrap.md), `checks/run.mjs --init` | adoption and the idempotent per-repo re-run |

**The test for "is this core?"** — would *every* pack's content stop working without it? The
planner, the runner, the migration mechanism, the orchestrator loop all pass; a lint for one
technology, a nightly release task, a naming rule all fail. Two responsibilities are core *by
ownership* even though they run as pack tasks: **baselining** (the baseline pack's daily task) and the
**daily-run** itself are Claudinite's job, not a pack's — the pack is only the delivery slot.

## What a pack contributes

A pack is a directory `packs/<name>/pack.mjs` exporting contribution slots (any subset
— a pack may carry only prose, only a task, only checks):

| Slot | Field | Carries |
|---|---|---|
| **Prose** | `prose: 'RULES.md'` | always-relevant-to-a-project guidance, injected into context when the pack is active |
| **Checks** | `rules: [...]` | deterministic conformance rules run at every Stop and in CI |
| **Skills** | `skills: [...]` | activity-scoped procedures mounted wherever the pack is declared |
| **Daily tasks** | `run_daily: [...]` | `(gate, worker)` maintenance units the planner picks up — each declares `full_sweep_supported` and its `smarts` tier |
| **Questions** | `questions: [...]` | mandatory adoption-interview questions; the owner's answers live verbatim on the project's pack entry ([packs/README.md](packs/README.md#adoption-interview-questions)) |

Activation is the project's declaration in `.claudinite-checks.json` — **no pack runs undeclared,
the baseline included.** A technology pack carries a `detect` fingerprint so `--init` seeds it into a
fresh declaration when the technology is present; the marker only *suspects* a pack is wanted,
never forcing or forbidding its declaration afterward. A declared-by-policy pack (the baseline and
the default-on maintenance packs) sets `detect: null` and is seeded by `--init`
and/or a one-time migration.

### Two homes for a pack: the canon, and a project's own `local_packs/`

A pack contributes the same slots from either of two homes, and the engine runs both the
same way:

- **A canon pack** — `packs/<name>/` in this repo, mounted read-only into every consumer. It is
  *portable*: written as if no one project existed, shared by every project that declares it.
- **A local pack** — `.claudinite/local_packs/<name>/` in a **consumer's own tree**, tracked
  project content the consumer authors and commits. It is *project-specific*: the working style,
  values, checks, and skills that don't generalize past this one repo — the project's
  **normalized capture surface** (what used to sprawl as always-loaded `CLAUDE.md`/`dev/procedures`
  prose). `discoverPacks` scans both roots; a local pack carries its own `dir`, is `local: true`,
  and may not shadow a canon id. Prose injection, the Stop/CI checks, and skill mounting treat a
  declared local pack exactly like a canon one. (A local pack may also declare `run_daily` tasks, and
  the fleet planner has a tested seam for them, but that daily-run path is **experimental and not
  enabled by default** — see [packs/README.md](packs/README.md); scheduled work stays a canon-pack
  task or an out-of-repo routine for now.)

The split is the same **portable-vs-specific** line the growth lifecycle already draws: a rule true
beyond this project belongs in a canon pack (proposed by PR, or promoted up by the growth routine);
a rule specific to this project belongs in its local pack. Neither is ever "hardcode it into the
engine."

## Where a new feature goes — the routing

Ask what *kind* of thing you're adding; each kind has exactly one home, and none of them is core:

1. **A new rule or practice** → the [promotion ladder](checks/DESIGN.md) (platform setting →
   PreToolUse hook → post-hoc check → skill → prose) picks the mechanism; it lands in a pack (or
   a skill that pack requires). *Which* pack follows the portable-vs-specific split: a portable rule
   → a canon pack; a rule specific to one project → that project's own `local_packs/` pack. The
   ladder owns *which* mechanism — this doc only says the answer is never "hardcode it into the
   engine," and (for a project-specific rule) never "always-loaded `CLAUDE.md` prose" when a local
   pack's check or skill can carry it.
2. **A new technology's conventions** → a new technology pack, with a `detect` fingerprint so
   `--init` seeds it when the technology is present (declaring it stays the project's call).
3. **A new scheduled maintenance behavior** → a `run_daily` task (a `(gate, worker)` pair) on the
   owning pack. The planner assembles it automatically — **no edit to the orchestrator or the
   planner.** This is the load-bearing case: "add a nightly job" must never become "add a routine
   to `routines/`."
4. **A new project-class playbook** → a project-class pack.
5. **Extending the engine itself** — a new signal the planner computes, a new discovery rule, a
   new migration capability, a change to the orchestrator loop — *is* the rare core change. Do it
   deliberately, and expect it to serve every pack, not one.

If a proposed change is a new file under `routines/`, `checks/` (beyond the runner/lib), or a new
branch in the planner *for one task's sake*, stop: it almost certainly belongs in a pack.

## Relocating into a pack: retire the old home

Most "make this a pack feature" work is a **move** — a rule out of always-loaded prose and into a
check, a routine into a pack's `run_daily`, a doc into a skill. A move isn't finished when the new
home works; it's finished when the **old home is gone**. Leaving the original as a tombstone stub
(`# retired — see the pack`) creates two homes for one thing, which drifts and misleads. Delete
the emptied source in the same change, fix every inbound reference, and let the conformance checks
(`reference-integrity`, `catalog-completeness`) confirm nothing dangles. The mechanics live in the
text-sweep skill; the discipline is: a relocation that
leaves the source behind isn't done.

---

Related: the corpus map and the two content homes are in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md); the mechanism
promotion ladder is in [checks/DESIGN.md](checks/DESIGN.md); keeping a canon change from hurting
consumers is [consumer-safe-changes.md](consumer-safe-changes.md).

content.subject: Phase 1: codify pack-independence rule (check + ordering check-the-work test + docs)
content.description: New basics check: a pack's code may import only its own files and the always-vendored engine surface (checks/lib, packs//skills/ root machinery) — never another pack's code or canon-internal trees. Cross-pack abilities flow through declaration + configuration. Includes the check-the-work test verifying (on the branch commit log) that the rule commit precedes the fix commits, per the owner's ordering meta-rule; plus DESIGN/extending doc updates. Own commit.

content.subject: Phase 2: fix the cross-pack misuse via declaration + configuration
content.description: Barriers pack gains contributed-barriers ability (packs contribute fixed barrier rules as data via pack.mjs `contributes`); basics + product-wiki require barriers and contribute their isolation barriers as config, dropping the defineBarrier imports; move `under` to checks/lib; move migrationActive to checks/lib so chrome-extension-release stops importing migrations/registry.mjs; update stale docs. Own commit.

content.subject: Phase 3: relevant migration fix (vendor coherence + consumer note)
content.description: Drop the import-closure approach (rebuild branch from origin/main); vendor set now carries barriers via the requires closure. Add real-corpus regression test (basics/product-wiki vendor sets include packs/barriers, import-closed) and a computeVendorSet coherence validation that errors on escaping imports; dated migration note for consumers whose local packs composed on the removed defineBarrier export. Own commit. Then update PR #348 + issue #349.

content.text: Red demonstrated on exactly the four outlawed sites, unit tests green (the ordering test skips until the rule commit exists). Committing Phase 1:

input.command:
git add checks/lib/imports.mjs packs/basics/pack-independence.mjs packs/basics/pack-independence.test.mjs packs/basics/pack.mjs extending.md checks/DESIGN.md && git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
Codify pack independence: no cross-pack code imports — abilities via declaration + config

The rule (extending.md): a pack's code imports only its own files and the
engine surface (checks/, mount/, the packs//skills/ root machinery) — never
another pack's code, and never a canon-internal tree. Another pack's abilities
arrive by declaring the dependency (requires) and passing configuration; a
helper both sides need moves into checks/lib. Rationale: the vendor set ships
a pack only when declared, so a cross-pack import crashes every consumer that
vendors one pack without the other — the exact failure the gated
vendored-mount flip aborted on for GoogleCalendarEventCreator (#349).

Enforced as the baseline pack-independence check (blocking), judging code
references tree-oracle style: an import specifier that resolves into another
pack's directory — canon packs and consumer local packs alike. checks/lib/
imports.mjs is the new one home for the import-surface facts (specifier scan,
resolution, the engine-surface definition) so the check and the vendor
writer's coherence guard can never drift apart.

Per the ordering meta-rule now recorded in checks/DESIGN.md — a rule that
mandates "X before Y" ships a check-the-work verification of that order — the
check's test file carries a branch-commit-log test asserting the commit
introducing this rule is an ancestor of each commit fixing an outlawed import
site. At this commit the check deliberately fires on the four live misuse
sites (basics, product-wiki x2, chrome-extension-release); the fixes land in
the follow-up commits, rule first — the mandated order the test verifies.

Refs #349

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01DJX6fa8A293pv5jK4ucAXy
EOF

1	import { finding } from '../../checks/lib/findings.mjs';
2	
3	// The barriers detection engine — language-agnostic enforcement of a directed
4	// folder-access graph. A *barrier edge* forbids the files under one set of
5	// folders (`from`) from referencing another (`to`); the engine finds every
6	// crossing reference.
7	//
8	// The one idea that keeps it precise and technology-agnostic: **the repo tree is
9	// the oracle.** A candidate reference (an import specifier, a path in a comment, a
10	// bare filename) only counts when it *resolves to a real tracked path* inside the
11	// barred folder. An English word that merely happens to be a folder's name never
12	// resolves, so it never fires — no per-language parser, no allowlist of file
13	// types, and near-zero false positives. See packs/barriers/README.md, including
14	// its "Known limitations" section for the reference forms this does not resolve.
15	// (One deliberate exception: with `matchNames: true` an edge opts into matching
16	// the bare *names* of its barred folders — restricted to distinctive names so
17	// prose can't false-positive; see the names layer below.)
18	//
19	// A rule owns its exceptions. Each `except` entry is either a structural
20	// carve-out (a folder/glob/pattern string that removes files from the guarded
21	// region — the `from: "."` helper) or a reviewed exception ({ path, to?, reason }
22	// — a specific file's deliberate crossing, staleness-audited when `to` is pinned).
23	//
24	// Exported for composition: another pack imports `defineBarrier` (or the lower
25	// `normalizeEdges` + `barrierFindings`) and contributes a fixed barrier as one of
26	// its own `rules`, the same way packs already share the checks/ engine lib.
27	
28	export const DEFAULT_DOC = 'packs/barriers/README.md';
29	
30	// Test files are out of scope by nature: a test references what it tests, so a
31	// core test naming pack/skill content is expected, not a coupling. The engine
32	// never scans them — the project doesn't carve them out rule by rule.
33	const isTestFile = (f) => /\.test\.[cm]?js$/.test(f);
34	
35	// --- path helpers (posix; both separators accepted on input) ----------------
36	
37	// A folder/target prefix as the config author wrote it → a bare posix prefix:
38	// backslashes folded, a leading "./" or "/" and any trailing "/" stripped, and a
39	// lone "." (repo root) collapsed to "". '*' is the isolation wildcard and passes
40	// through untouched. A prefix that collapses to "" means the repo root; callers
41	// that can't accept it (targets, allow) reject it.

content.subject: Phase 1: codify pack-independence rule (check + ordering check-the-work test + docs)
content.description: New basics check: a pack's code may import only its own files and the always-vendored engine surface (checks/lib, packs//skills/ root machinery) — never another pack's code or canon-internal trees. Cross-pack abilities flow through declaration + configuration. Includes the check-the-work test verifying (on the branch commit log) that the rule commit precedes the fix commits, per the owner's ordering meta-rule; plus DESIGN/extending doc updates. Own commit.

content.subject: Phase 2: fix the cross-pack misuse via declaration + configuration
content.description: Barriers pack gains contributed-barriers ability (packs contribute fixed barrier rules as data via pack.mjs `contributes`); basics + product-wiki require barriers and contribute their isolation barriers as config, dropping the defineBarrier imports; move `under` to checks/lib; move migrationActive to checks/lib so chrome-extension-release stops importing migrations/registry.mjs; update stale docs. Own commit.

content.subject: Phase 3: relevant migration fix (vendor coherence + consumer note)
content.description: Drop the import-closure approach (rebuild branch from origin/main); vendor set now carries barriers via the requires closure. Add real-corpus regression test (basics/product-wiki vendor sets include packs/barriers, import-closed) and a computeVendorSet coherence validation that errors on escaping imports; dated migration note for consumers whose local packs composed on the removed defineBarrier export. Own commit. Then update PR #348 + issue #349.

lines: ## Composing a barrier from another pack
-The engine is exported so a pack can ship a *fixed* barrier as one of its own rules — no project config needed. Import `defineBarrier` and add the result to the pack's `rules`:

+A pack ships a *fixed* barrier — no project config needed — by **declaring** this pack and **contributing** the barrier as data on its manifest, never by importing this pack's code (`pack-independence`): name `barriers` in the pack's `requires` and carry the barrier under `contributes`:
```js
// packs/<somepack>/pack.mjs
-import { defineBarrier } from '../barriers/engine.mjs';
export default {
id: 'somepack',
requires: ['barriers'],
-  rules: [
-    defineBarrier({
+  contributes: {
+    barriers: [{
id: 'requirements-isolation',
edges: [{ from: 'dev/requirements', to: '*', reason: 'requirements is a pure sink' }],

-`engine.mjs` also exports the lower-level `normalizeEdges` and `barrierFindings` (which returns `{ findings, stale }`; every crossing finding carries its resolved path as `resolved`), plus `staleFindings` for a pack that needs finer control. This is the same "packs share the engine lib" pattern the `packs/` file-placement acceptance already covers.

+Each contribution becomes a **first-class rule under its own id** — per-rule overrides (`rules: { "requirements-isolation": "off" }`) and acceptances address it directly, and `description`/`why`/`doc`/`severity`/`crossingExcuse` ride the contribution. `gateDir` is the one declarative gate: the rule stays inert until that directory exists in the repo under test (how the baseline's consumer-isolation wall stays quiet pre-flip). The runner hands this pack the active-pack list (`contributedRules` on its manifest, the generic core seam), and [contributed.mjs](contributed.mjs) builds the rules — an undeclared pack contributes nothing, exactly as its own rules would not run. A consumer's local pack contributes the same way. A helper a pack needs beside a barrier (path containment, say) lives in the engine lib (`checks/lib/paths.mjs`), never in this pack's modules.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Claudinite — corpus index

> ℹ️ The owner's personal preferences and the active packs' prose are both injected automatically by
> SessionStart hooks — they're already in context above. Honor them; there is nothing to go read.

**Routing map, not a payload.** Two homes hold the corpus, selected by *when* a rule is active:

- **`packs/<name>/`** — a bundle of prose (a pack's `RULES.md`) **and** checks, selected **once per
  session** by the project's declaration in `.claudinite-checks.json`. No pack is active by default —
  `basics` too is declared explicitly (bootstrap seeds it). The active packs' prose loads at session
  start via `packs/load-active-prose.mjs`; the checks run at every Stop and in CI. This is the "decided
  once, seldom changes" set. A pack can live here (the shared, portable canon) **or** in a consumer's
  own `.claudinite/local_packs/<name>/` (tracked project content — its project-specific packs, the same
  slots); the engine discovers and runs both the same way ([extending.md](extending.md)).
- **`skills/<name>/`** — activity-scoped procedures, surfaced **on demand** by the harness when the work
  in front of you matches the skill. Deployment is pack-driven: each pack declares the skills it
  requires, and a repo mounts the union over its active packs at session start
  (`skills/mount-skills.mjs`); the `skill-ownership` check keeps every skill required by some pack.
  Catalog: [skills/README.md](skills/README.md).

Everything enforceable is a check or a hook; everything always-relevant-to-a-project is pack prose;
everything activity-scoped is a skill. Before adding *any* rule as prose, run the promotion ladder in
[checks/DESIGN.md](checks/DESIGN.md): a platform setting, a hook, a check, or a skill that can carry it
beats prose. And before adding *any* feature — a rule, a technology's conventions, a nightly task —
decide the core/pack boundary first: [extending.md](extending.md) is the map of what's engine and what's
pack-contributed content, and where each kind of feature goes (almost never core).

## packs/ — prose + checks, active only when declared (`basics` too)

- **[packs/basics/](packs/basics/RULES.md)** — the baseline: working discipline, the task
  lifecycle, and the core checks. Declared explicitly like every other pack — bootstrap seeds
  the declaration, the nightly baselining backfills it into existing consumers.
- **[packs/barriers/](packs/barriers/README.md)** — a mechanism pack: enforce a directed
  folder-access graph (folder A may not reference folder B — imports, path/filename references, in
  any language, comments and docs included), declared per-project as `config` on the repo's barriers pack entry. Its
  detection engine is exported so other packs compose their own separation rules on it (the way
  `spec-driven-product` builds on `executable-requirements`).
- **Technology packs**, active when the project declares them (bootstrap's `--init` seeds the
  declaration from a fingerprint): `chrome-extension` (MV3 coding gotchas, fingerprinted by the
  manifest), `chrome-extension-release` (the *opt-in* release/store standard in its
  [RELEASE.md](packs/chrome-extension-release/RELEASE.md) + the conformance checks — declared when the
  project is ready to ship, fingerprinted by its single `Release to Chrome Store` workflow stub), `github-actions`
  (workflow lints), `node`, `aws-sam`, `html`, `flutter`, `firebase` (rules/functions/deploy
  discipline, fingerprinted by `firebase.json`) with its opt-in `firebase-release` (dev/prod
  project split + App Check store gating — declared near shipping), and the stubs `android`,
  `ios`, `play-store-release`, `app-store-release` (filled when first exercised).
- **Project-class packs**, declared by kind of project: `research-project` (the
  algorithm-iteration playbook — [packs/research-project/RULES.md](packs/research-project/RULES.md))
  and `spec-driven-product` (the executable-spec product playbook —
  [packs/spec-driven-product/RULES.md](packs/spec-driven-product/RULES.md)) with its mechanics
  companion `executable-requirements` (the framework standard — layout, gates, kinds incl. the
  storyboard `saga` kind, gallery, determinism; fingerprinted by `dev/requirements/requirements.md`
  — [packs/executable-requirements/RULES.md](packs/executable-requirements/RULES.md)); and
  `product-wiki` (the self-growing product research wiki standard — layout, section/citation/log
  checks, a fixed isolation barrier, a weekly growth daily task; fingerprinted by
  `product-wiki/product-requirements/README.md` — [packs/product-wiki/README.md](packs/product-wiki/README.md)).

The declaration is **pack-oriented**: a `packs` entry is a pack id, or an entry object carrying that
pack's own settings — its parameters (`config`), its adoption-interview answers (`answers` — a pack
may declare the questions its adoption must ask; the unanswered gap surfaces as a mild SessionStart
note, strict only inside bootstrap), and the rule overrides/acceptances its declaration
motivates (`rules`/`accept`, the entry being their provenance). Top-level `rules`/`accept` stay for
project-wide decisions and skill-owned checks. Full schema: [checks/README.md](checks/README.md).

**Settings validity** — an unknown pack name, an unknown property (top-level or on a pack entry), or
malformed JSON in `.claudinite-checks.json` — is checked when the file loads and surfaced by the runner as
a blocking `config` error (a wrong pack name is as much a settings error as bad JSON), not a conformance
check among the packs. A pack's `marker` only *suspects* the pack is wanted; whether to declare it is the
project's call, so neither a marker without its declaration nor a declaration without its marker is flagged.

A pack that only makes sense alongside another names it in its `requires` list (e.g.
`chrome-extension-release` requires `chrome-extension`, `firebase-release` requires `firebase`,
`spec-driven-product` requires `executable-requirements`). This isn't a check: a pack can't be imported
without its dependencies, so `resolveDeclaredPacks` ([packs/registry.mjs](packs/registry.mjs)) pulls each
declared pack's `requires` closure into the declaration when it's written — at `--init` and the baselining
backfill — materializing the prerequisite in the file, visible like every other entry, as
`{ "id": ..., "via": [...] }` with `via` naming the packs that require it.

## skills/ — activity-scoped procedures, surfaced on demand

The full catalog and each skill's trigger live in [skills/README.md](skills/README.md) — the source of
truth, kept complete against the tree by the `catalog-completeness` check. Two kinds: **command skills**
(owner-phrase or bootstrap triggers — e.g. `merge-to-main`, `lessons-learned`, `bump-version`) and
**practice skills** (surfaced by the activity in front of you — e.g. `bug-investigation`,
`writing-tests`, `engineering-practices`, `file-placement`). See the catalog for the complete list.

## preferences/ — auto-injected by the SessionStart hook

`preferences/<email>.md` holds the owner's per-user interaction preferences. The
`mount/inject-preferences.sh` SessionStart step loads the current user's file into context — you
don't read it yourself.

---

Repo internals — what this repo is, how consumers mount it, the maintenance routines → [README.md](README.md).
Changing the canon without hurting consuming repos → [consumer-safe-changes.md](consumer-safe-changes.md) (provisional).

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Extending Claudinite — the core/pack boundary and where a feature goes

Before adding *anything* to this repo, decide which of two layers it belongs to. Getting this
wrong is the recurring failure mode — a rule, a technology's conventions, or a nightly task
wired straight into the engine instead of contributed as pack content, where every consumer then
pays for it whether or not they need it.

## The one principle

> **Core is the engine that runs pack-contributed content. A feature is core only if it *is* the
> engine.** Everything a project could plausibly not need — a rule, a technology's gotchas, a
> maintenance task, a project-class playbook — is pack content, discovered structurally and
> activated by declaration. If you can't say "this is machinery every pack relies on," it isn't
> core.

This is the same instinct the corpus already applies to itself: packs and skills are discovered
by *scanning the tree*, not by a hand-maintained list ([packs/registry.mjs](packs/registry.mjs),
[skills/registry.mjs](skills/registry.mjs)); the mechanism is fixed, the content is open. New
content drops in a directory; new mechanism is a deliberate change to the engine.

## What is core (the engine)

Core is the small, closed set of machinery that runs, discovers, and schedules content. Touch it
only to extend the *mechanism*, never to add one project's rule or task:

| Engine piece | Home | What it is |
|---|---|---|
| Checks runner + hooks | [`checks/`](checks/README.md) | the dependency-free runner, its lib, the Stop hook, the PreToolUse guard — runs the packs' checks; owns no rule itself |
| Pack discovery + prose injection | `packs/registry.mjs`, `packs/load-active-prose.mjs` | structural scan of `packs/*/pack.mjs`; SessionStart injection of active packs' prose |
| Skill discovery + mounting | `skills/registry.mjs`, `skills/mount-skills.mjs` | structural scan; per-session symlink of the active packs' skill union |
| Adoption interviews | `packs/interview.mjs` | the gap computation (a pack's declared questions minus the entry's stored answers) and the SessionStart nudge; owns no question itself |
| Baseline-migration mechanism | [`migrations/`](migrations/README.md) | the read-side resolver, write-side rename, and fleet telemetry that auto-retires a relocation once every consumer has moved |
| The run_daily planner | [`routines/fleet/`](routines/fleet/DESIGN.md) | goes over the reachable repos, assembles each one's due tasks from its active packs, masks full-sweep, isolates a throwing gate, emits the plan; pack-agnostic, owns no task, depends on no pack |
| The orchestrator | [`routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md`](routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md) | the single scheduled entry point — runs the planner over the accessible fleet, reads the plan, fans out the units |
| Bootstrap / baselining | [`bootstrap.md`](bootstrap.md), `checks/run.mjs --init` | adoption and the idempotent per-repo re-run |

**The test for "is this core?"** — would *every* pack's content stop working without it? The
planner, the runner, the migration mechanism, the orchestrator loop all pass; a lint for one
technology, a nightly release task, a naming rule all fail. Two responsibilities are core *by
ownership* even though they run as pack tasks: **baselining** (the baseline pack's daily task) and the
**daily-run** itself are Claudinite's job, not a pack's — the pack is only the delivery slot.

## What a pack contributes

A pack is a directory `packs/<name>/pack.mjs` exporting contribution slots (any subset
— a pack may carry only prose, only a task, only checks):

| Slot | Field | Carries |
|---|---|---|
| **Prose** | `prose: 'RULES.md'` | always-relevant-to-a-project guidance, injected into context when the pack is active |
| **Checks** | `rules: [...]` | deterministic conformance rules run at every Stop and in CI |
| **Skills** | `skills: [...]` | activity-scoped procedures mounted wherever the pack is declared |
| **Daily tasks** | `run_daily: [...]` | `(gate, worker)` maintenance units the planner picks up — each declares `full_sweep_supported` and its `smarts` tier |
| **Questions** | `questions: [...]` | mandatory adoption-interview questions; the owner's answers live verbatim on the project's pack entry ([packs/README.md](packs/README.md#adoption-interview-questions)) |

**Packs are independent.** A pack's code imports only its **own** files and the engine surface
(`checks/`, `mount/`, the machinery `.mjs` at the `packs/`/`skills/` roots) — never another
pack's code, and never a canon-internal tree (`migrations/`, `routines/`): the vendor set ships
a pack only when declared and ships no canon-internal tree at all, so such an import crashes
every consumer that vendors the importer without its target. A pack that wants another pack's
*abilities* declares the dependency (`requires`) and passes **configuration**; a helper both
sides need moves into `checks/lib`. Enforced by the baseline `pack-independence` check.

Activation is the project's declaration in `.claudinite-checks.json` — **no pack runs undeclared,
the baseline included.** A technology pack carries a `detect` fingerprint so `--init` seeds it into a
fresh declaration when the technology is present; the marker only *suspects* a pack is wanted,
never forcing or forbidding its declaration afterward. A declared-by-policy pack (the baseline and
the default-on maintenance packs) sets `detect: null` and is seeded by `--init`
and/or a one-time migration.

### Two homes for a pack: the canon, and a project's own `local_packs/`

A pack contributes the same slots from either of two homes, and the engine runs both the
same way:

- **A canon pack** — `packs/<name>/` in this repo, mounted read-only into every consumer. It is
  *portable*: written as if no one project existed, shared by every project that declares it.
- **A local pack** — `.claudinite/local_packs/<name>/` in a **consumer's own tree**, tracked
  project content the consumer authors and commits. It is *project-specific*: the working style,
  values, checks, and skills that don't generalize past this one repo — the project's
  **normalized capture surface** (what used to sprawl as always-loaded `CLAUDE.md`/`dev/procedures`
  prose). `discoverPacks` scans both roots; a local pack carries its own `dir`, is `local: true`,
  and may not shadow a canon id. Prose injection, the Stop/CI checks, and skill mounting treat a
  declared local pack exactly like a canon one. (A local pack may also declare `run_daily` tasks, and
  the fleet planner has a tested seam for them, but that daily-run path is **experimental and not
  enabled by default** — see [packs/README.md](packs/README.md); scheduled work stays a canon-pack
  task or an out-of-repo routine for now.)

The split is the same **portable-vs-specific** line the growth lifecycle already draws: a rule true
beyond this project belongs in a canon pack (proposed by PR, or promoted up by the growth routine);
a rule specific to this project belongs in its local pack. Neither is ever "hardcode it into the
engine."

## Where a new feature goes — the routing

Ask what *kind* of thing you're adding; each kind has exactly one home, and none of them is core:

1. **A new rule or practice** → the [promotion ladder](checks/DESIGN.md) (platform setting →
   PreToolUse hook → post-hoc check → skill → prose) picks the mechanism; it lands in a pack (or
   a skill that pack requires). *Which* pack follows the portable-vs-specific split: a portable rule
   → a canon pack; a rule specific to one project → that project's own `local_packs/` pack. The
   ladder owns *which* mechanism — this doc only says the answer is never "hardcode it into the
   engine," and (for a project-specific rule) never "always-loaded `CLAUDE.md` prose" when a local
   pack's check or skill can carry it.
2. **A new technology's conventions** → a new technology pack, with a `detect` fingerprint so
   `--init` seeds it when the technology is present (declaring it stays the project's call).
3. **A new scheduled maintenance behavior** → a `run_daily` task (a `(gate, worker)` pair) on the
   owning pack. The planner assembles it automatically — **no edit to the orchestrator or the
   planner.** This is the load-bearing case: "add a nightly job" must never become "add a routine
   to `routines/`."
4. **A new project-class playbook** → a project-class pack.
5. **Extending the engine itself** — a new signal the planner computes, a new discovery rule, a
   new migration capability, a change to the orchestrator loop — *is* the rare core change. Do it
   deliberately, and expect it to serve every pack, not one.

If a proposed change is a new file under `routines/`, `checks/` (beyond the runner/lib), or a new
branch in the planner *for one task's sake*, stop: it almost certainly belongs in a pack.

## Relocating into a pack: retire the old home

Most "make this a pack feature" work is a **move** — a rule out of always-loaded prose and into a
check, a routine into a pack's `run_daily`, a doc into a skill. A move isn't finished when the new
home works; it's finished when the **old home is gone**. Leaving the original as a tombstone stub
(`# retired — see the pack`) creates two homes for one thing, which drifts and misleads. Delete
the emptied source in the same change, fix every inbound reference, and let the conformance checks
(`reference-integrity`, `catalog-completeness`) confirm nothing dangles. The mechanics live in the
text-sweep skill; the discipline is: a relocation that
leaves the source behind isn't done.

---

Related: the corpus map and the two content homes are in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md); the mechanism
promotion ladder is in [checks/DESIGN.md](checks/DESIGN.md); keeping a canon change from hurting
consumers is [consumer-safe-changes.md](consumer-safe-changes.md).

content.subject: Phase 1: codify pack-independence rule (check + ordering check-the-work test + docs)
content.description: New basics check: a pack's code may import only its own files and the always-vendored engine surface (checks/lib, packs//skills/ root machinery) — never another pack's code or canon-internal trees. Cross-pack abilities flow through declaration + configuration. Includes the check-the-work test verifying (on the branch commit log) that the rule commit precedes the fix commits, per the owner's ordering meta-rule; plus DESIGN/extending doc updates. Own commit.

content.subject: Phase 2: fix the cross-pack misuse via declaration + configuration

Own commit.

content.subject: Phase 3: relevant migration fix (vendor coherence + consumer note)

Add real-corpus regression test (basics/product-wiki vendor sets include packs/barriers, import-closed) and a computeVendorSet coherence validation that errors on escaping imports; dated migration note for consumers whose local packs composed on the removed defineBarrier export. Own commit. Then update PR #348 + issue #349.

content.subject: Phase 1: codify pack-independence rule (check + ordering check-the-work test + docs)
content.description: New basics check: a pack's code may import only its own files and the always-vendored engine surface (checks/lib, packs//skills/ root machinery) — never another pack's code or canon-internal trees. Cross-pack abilities flow through declaration + configuration. Includes the check-the-work test verifying (on the branch commit log) that the rule commit precedes the fix commits, per the owner's ordering meta-rule; plus DESIGN/extending doc updates. Own commit.

content.subject: Phase 2: fix the cross-pack misuse via declaration + configuration
content.description: Barriers pack gains contributed-barriers ability (packs contribute fixed barrier rules as data via pack.mjs `contributes`); basics + product-wiki require barriers and contribute their isolation barriers as config, dropping the defineBarrier imports; move `under` to checks/lib; move migrationActive to checks/lib so chrome-extension-release stops importing migrations/registry.mjs; update stale docs. Own commit.

content.subject: Phase 3: relevant migration fix (vendor coherence + consumer note)
content.description: Drop the import-closure approach (rebuild branch from origin/main); vendor set now carries barriers via the requires closure. Add real-corpus regression test (basics/product-wiki vendor sets include packs/barriers, import-closed) and a computeVendorSet coherence validation that errors on escaping imports; dated migration note for consumers whose local packs composed on the removed defineBarrier export. Own commit. Then update PR #348 + issue #349.

input.command:
git add -A && git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
Fix the cross-pack misuse: barriers composed by contribution, helpers to the engine lib

The four imports pack-independence outlawed, fixed the way the rule mandates —
declaration + configuration, never code:

- The barriers pack gains the contribution route: a pack declares
  requires: ['barriers'] and carries a fixed barrier as manifest DATA
  (contributes: { barriers: [{ id, edges, gateDir?, ... }] });
  packs/barriers/contributed.mjs builds each contribution into a first-class
  rule under the contribution's own id, so per-rule overrides and acceptances
  keep addressing claudinite-isolation and product-wiki-isolation exactly as
  before. gateDir carries the vendored-mount gate declaratively. The removed
  defineBarrier export was the code-composition route this replaces.
- basics and product-wiki now require barriers and contribute their isolation
  walls as data; their isolation modules become pure data. The requires
  closure materializes the barriers declaration (--init now writes
  { id: 'barriers', via: ['basics'] }; baselining backfills consumers).
- checks/run.mjs gains the generic contributedRules seam: each active pack
  interprets the other ACTIVE packs' contributions addressed to it —
  fail-soft per pack, contributed rules listed by --list, an undeclared pack
  neither runs nor contributes. Core stays pack-agnostic.
- Shared helpers move to the engine lib, the one surface every consumer
  vendors: under/normPrefix to checks/lib/paths.mjs (product-wiki imported
  them from the barriers engine), the sync migration surface
  (migrationActive) to checks/lib/migrations.mjs (chrome-extension-release
  imported migrations/registry.mjs, which is never vendored — in a vendored
  consumer the absent migrations/ tree now answers "no active migrations",
  correct for a flipped member). migrations/registry.mjs re-exports.
- Adoption interviews skip a via-materialized dependency until the project
  engages with it (config or answers on the entry) — a mechanism pack pulled
  in by the baseline must not nag every consumer with its adoption questions.

Known one-cycle window: a consumer whose declaration predates the backfill has
no barriers entry yet, so the contributed walls sit dormant there until its
nightly baselining materializes the requires closure — the standard canon-
change exposure (mount/DESIGN.md trade-off 10), self-healing.

Refs #349

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01DJX6fa8A293pv5jK4ucAXy
EOF
git log --oneline -2

lines: +  // Coherence guard: the set must be import-closed — every relative import in
+  // every .mjs it carries resolves to a file it also carries. Structural
+  // discovery plus the requires closure make that true by construction while
+  // the corpus honors pack-independence (a pack imports only its own files and
+  // the engine surface, both always in the set); a violation is canon-side
+  // breakage, reported here so convergence aborts BEFORE any write (the
+  // transactional contract) instead of the flipped member crashing on a
+  // missing module — the failure the gated flip's pilot abort surfaced.
+  const inSet = new Set(files);
+  for (const file of inSet) {
+    if (!file.endsWith('.mjs')) continue;
+    let src;
+    try { src = readFileSync(join(canonRoot, file), 'utf8'); } catch { continue; }
+    for (const { spec } of relativeImports(src)) {
+      const resolved = resolveRelative(file, spec, (p) => existsSync(join(canonRoot, p)));
+      if (!resolved) {
+        errors.push({
+          what: `${file} imports "${spec}", which resolves to no file in the canon tree`,
+          fix: 'fix the import specifier, or restore the file it names',
+      } else if (!inSet.has(resolved)) {
+        errors.push({
+          what: `${file} imports "${spec}" → ${resolved}, which the vendor set does not carry — a pack imports only its own files and the engine surface (pack-independence)`,
+          fix: 'fix the import to honor pack-independence (declare the dependency and contribute configuration, or move the helper into checks/lib)',
return { files: [...files].sort(), errors };

Vendored-mount fix: verify the vendor set import-closed; consumer conversion note

The migration-side fix for the GCEC pilot abort (#349). With the pack fix in
place the vendor set is coherent by construction — basics/product-wiki pull
the barriers pack through the requires closure computeVendorSet already
resolves — so the vendor computation now VERIFIES that instead of patching
around incoherence: after building the set, every vendored module's relative
imports must resolve within it, judged against the same engine-surface
definitions the pack-independence check enforces (checks/lib/imports.mjs —
one home; mount/vendor.mjs re-exports the layout constants from it). A
violation is reported in errors, so convergence aborts before any write (the
transactional contract) with a message naming the offending import, instead
of a flipped member crashing on a missing module and the worker aborting a
night later.

Regression test pinned to the failure: the basics and product-wiki vendor
sets carry the barriers pack and compute with zero errors — the exact sets
the GoogleCalendarEventCreator flip found broken.

The dated note (migrations/active_migrations/2026-07-19-pack-independence.mjs)
carries the one-time consumer-side guidance: a member's local pack that
composed a barrier by importing the shared engine converts to the
contributes-on-manifest shape; no mechanical ops, auto-retires.

Closes #349

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01DJX6fa8A293pv5jK4ucAXy
EOF

input.title: Pack independence: no cross-pack code imports — compose by declaration + contributed config (fixes the GCEC vendored-tree abort)
input.body:
## What broke

Nightly baselining of `GoogleCalendarEventCreator` hit the gated vendored-mount flip and found the vendored tree would be incoherent: `computeVendorSet` omitted `packs/barriers/` while `basics`/`product-wiki` imported `../barriers/engine.mjs`, so the flipped member would crash on the missing import. The worker aborted safely; the bug recurs nightly until fixed (#349).

## The fix — three phases, one commit each (rule first, by owner direction)

**1. Codify the rule** (`444cb5b`): a pack's code imports only its **own** files and the engine surface (`checks/`, `mount/`, the `packs/`/`skills/` root machinery) — never another pack's code, never a canon-internal tree. Another pack's *abilities* arrive by declaring it (`requires`) and passing configuration. Enforced as the baseline **`pack-independence`** check (blocking, tree-oracle import resolution, covers consumer local packs too); `checks/lib/imports.mjs` is the one home for the import-surface facts. Per the ordering meta-rule recorded in `checks/DESIGN.md` — a rule mandating "X before Y" ships a check-the-work verification — the check's test file asserts **on the branch commit log** that the rule commit is an ancestor of each misuse-fix commit. At this commit the check deliberately fired on the four live misuse sites.

**2. Fix the misuse** (`85ab5aa`): the barriers pack gains the **contribution route** — a pack declares `requires: ['barriers']` and carries a fixed barrier as manifest **data** (`contributes: { barriers: [...] }`); `contributed.mjs` builds each into a first-class rule under the contribution's own id, so existing accepts/overrides for `claudinite-isolation` and `product-wiki-isolation` keep working (`gateDir` carries the vendored-mount gate declaratively; `defineBarrier` is gone). `basics`/`product-wiki` contribute their walls as data; `under`/`normPrefix` move to `checks/lib/paths.mjs`; the sync migration surface (`migrationActive`) moves to `checks/lib/migrations.mjs` (closing the latent `chrome-extension-release → migrations/registry.mjs` escape — in a vendored consumer the absent tree answers "no active migrations", correct for a flipped member). `checks/run.mjs` gains the generic `contributedRules` seam; adoption interviews skip `via`-materialized dependencies until the project engages with them.

**3. Migration fix** (`d2c0a39`): the vendor set is coherent by construction now (the requires closure pulls barriers in — this alone unblocks the GCEC flip), and `computeVendorSet` **verifies** it: every vendored module's relative imports must resolve within the set, judged against the same engine-surface definitions the check enforces; a violation aborts convergence before any write, naming the offending import. Regression test pinned to the failure: the `basics` and `product-wiki` vendor sets carry the barriers pack with zero errors. A dated migration note carries the one-time consumer guidance (local packs that composed on the removed export convert to `contributes`).

## Known one-cycle window

A consumer whose declaration predates the backfill has no `barriers` entry yet, so the contributed walls sit dormant there until its nightly baselining materializes the requires closure — the standard canon-change exposure (mount/DESIGN.md, trade-off 10), self-healing.

## Verification

- 359 tests green (new: check unit tests, contribution factory + gateDir + malformed-contribution, runner seam end-to-end, interview via-gating, vendor coherence guard + real-corpus regression).
- The check-the-work ordering test asserts for real on this branch: rule commit ≺ fix commits.
- `checks/run.mjs` exits clean; at phase 1 it demonstrably fired on all four outlawed sites (see-it-fail).
- All-packs vendor audit: every declarable pack's set import-closed, zero errors.

input.body:
## Symptom

Nightly baselining of `GoogleCalendarEventCreator` hit the gated vendored-mount flip and found the vendored tree would be broken: `computeVendorSet` omits `packs/barriers/` while `basics`/`product-wiki` import `../barriers/engine.mjs`, so the flipped member would crash on the missing import. The worker aborted safely and restored GCEC to its coherent pre-flip state, but it's a canon-side bug that recurs every night until fixed.

## Root cause (revised per owner direction)

The imports themselves are the bug, not the vendor computation: packs must not refer to each other's code at all. The `basics`/`product-wiki` → `barriers/engine.mjs` composition imports (and the latent `chrome-extension-release` → `migrations/registry.mjs` import of the same class) coupled packs at the module level, which no vendor-set computation can make coherent without shipping undeclared code.

## Fix (PR #348, three phases — rule first)

1. **Rule**: the baseline `pack-independence` check — a pack's code imports only its own files and the engine surface; another pack's abilities arrive via `requires` + configuration. With a check-the-work test verifying on the branch commit log that the rule landed before the fixes.
2. **Pack fix**: barriers composition becomes declaration + contributed manifest data (`contributes: { barriers: [...] }` interpreted by the barriers pack via the runner's generic `contributedRules` seam); shared helpers move into `checks/lib` (the vendored engine surface).
3. **Migration fix**: the vendor set is coherent via the requires closure (this unblocks the GCEC flip), and `computeVendorSet` now verifies the set import-closed, aborting before any write with the offending import named; a dated note guides consumer local packs off the removed `defineBarrier` export.

Comment class: process-change (the pack-independence rule + the ordering meta-rule, both now codified), correction (the import-closure fix replaced by declaration + configuration)

Done — the rule you stated is codified, the misuse is fixed the way the rule mandates, and the phases are separate commits on PR [#348](https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/pull/348) (rule first, as directed):

**`444cb5b` — the rule.** Baseline `pack-independence` check (blocking): a pack's code imports only its own files and the engine surface (`checks/`, `mount/`, root machinery); another pack's abilities arrive via `requires` + configuration. Covers consumer local packs too. The law is in `extending.md`; your meta-rule — *any rule stating "X before Y" ships a check-the-work test* — is recorded in `checks/DESIGN.md`, and this rule's ordering ("codify before fixing") is verified by a **check-the-work test on the branch commit log**: the rule commit must be an ancestor of each misuse-fix commit. At this commit the check demonstrably fired on all four live sites (see-it-fail).

**`85ab5aa` — the pack fix.** Barriers gains the configuration route: `contributes: { barriers: [{ id, edges, gateDir?, … }] }` on a pack's manifest, interpreted through a generic runner seam into first-class rules — `claudinite-isolation` and `product-wiki-isolation` keep their ids, so existing accepts/overrides keep working. `basics`/`product-wiki` now `require` barriers and contribute their walls as pure data; `defineBarrier` is removed; shared helpers (`under`, `migrationActive`) moved into `checks/lib` — which also closed the latent `chrome-extension-release → migrations/registry.mjs` escape of the same class.

**`d2c0a39` — the migration fix.** The requires closure now makes GCEC's vendor set carry barriers by construction (this alone unblocks the nightly flip), and `computeVendorSet` *verifies* the set is import-closed — judged against the same engine-surface definitions as the check, aborting before any write with the offending import named. Plus a dated note guiding consumer local packs off the removed export.

**Verified**: 359 tests green; conformance clean; the ordering test asserts for real (rule ≺ fixes confirmed on this branch's log); all-packs vendor audit import-closed with zero errors. Issue #349 updated with the revised root cause.

> ✅ **All done.** Three commits pushed (rule → pack fix → migration fix), PR #348 ready for review, nothing outstanding. One known self-healing window: consumers gain the materialized `barriers` declaration at their next nightly baselining; until then the contributed walls sit dormant there (standard one-cycle canon-change exposure).

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Claudinite — corpus index

> ℹ️ The owner's personal preferences and the active packs' prose are both injected automatically by
> SessionStart hooks — they're already in context above. Honor them; there is nothing to go read.

**Routing map, not a payload.** Two homes hold the corpus, selected by *when* a rule is active:

- **`packs/<name>/`** — a bundle of prose (a pack's `RULES.md`) **and** checks, selected **once per
  session** by the project's declaration in `.claudinite-checks.json`. No pack is active by default —
  `basics` too is declared explicitly (bootstrap seeds it). The active packs' prose loads at session
  start via `packs/load-active-prose.mjs`; the checks run at every Stop and in CI. This is the "decided
  once, seldom changes" set. A pack can live here (the shared, portable canon) **or** in a consumer's
  own `.claudinite/local_packs/<name>/` (tracked project content — its project-specific packs, the same
  slots); the engine discovers and runs both the same way ([extending.md](extending.md)).
- **`skills/<name>/`** — activity-scoped procedures, surfaced **on demand** by the harness when the work
  in front of you matches the skill. Deployment is pack-driven: each pack declares the skills it
  requires, and a repo mounts the union over its active packs at session start
  (`skills/mount-skills.mjs`); the `skill-ownership` check keeps every skill required by some pack.
  Catalog: [skills/README.md](skills/README.md).

Everything enforceable is a check or a hook; everything always-relevant-to-a-project is pack prose;
everything activity-scoped is a skill. Before adding *any* rule as prose, run the promotion ladder in
[checks/DESIGN.md](checks/DESIGN.md): a platform setting, a hook, a check, or a skill that can carry it
beats prose. And before adding *any* feature — a rule, a technology's conventions, a nightly task —
decide the core/pack boundary first: [extending.md](extending.md) is the map of what's engine and what's
pack-contributed content, and where each kind of feature goes (almost never core).

## packs/ — prose + checks, active only when declared (`basics` too)

- **[packs/basics/](packs/basics/RULES.md)** — the baseline: working discipline, the task
  lifecycle, and the core checks. Declared explicitly like every other pack — bootstrap seeds
  the declaration, the nightly baselining backfills it into existing consumers.
- **[packs/barriers/](packs/barriers/README.md)** — a mechanism pack: enforce a directed
  folder-access graph (folder A may not reference folder B — imports, path/filename references, in
  any language, comments and docs included), declared per-project as `config` on the repo's barriers pack entry.
  Other packs compose their own separation rules on it by *declaring* it (`requires`) and
  *contributing* a fixed barrier as manifest data (`contributes` — the way `basics` and
  `product-wiki` ship their isolation walls); no pack imports another's code (the baseline
  `pack-independence` check).
- **Technology packs**, active when the project declares them (bootstrap's `--init` seeds the
  declaration from a fingerprint): `chrome-extension` (MV3 coding gotchas, fingerprinted by the
  manifest), `chrome-extension-release` (the *opt-in* release/store standard in its
  [RELEASE.md](packs/chrome-extension-release/RELEASE.md) + the conformance checks — declared when the
  project is ready to ship, fingerprinted by its single `Release to Chrome Store` workflow stub), `github-actions`
  (workflow lints), `node`, `aws-sam`, `html`, `flutter`, `firebase` (rules/functions/deploy
  discipline, fingerprinted by `firebase.json`) with its opt-in `firebase-release` (dev/prod
  project split + App Check store gating — declared near shipping), and the stubs `android`,
  `ios`, `play-store-release`, `app-store-release` (filled when first exercised).
- **Project-class packs**, declared by kind of project: `research-project` (the
  algorithm-iteration playbook — [packs/research-project/RULES.md](packs/research-project/RULES.md))
  and `spec-driven-product` (the executable-spec product playbook —
  [packs/spec-driven-product/RULES.md](packs/spec-driven-product/RULES.md)) with its mechanics
  companion `executable-requirements` (the framework standard — layout, gates, kinds incl. the
  storyboard `saga` kind, gallery, determinism; fingerprinted by `dev/requirements/requirements.md`
  — [packs/executable-requirements/RULES.md](packs/executable-requirements/RULES.md)); and
  `product-wiki` (the self-growing product research wiki standard — layout, section/citation/log
  checks, a fixed isolation barrier, a weekly growth daily task; fingerprinted by
  `product-wiki/product-requirements/README.md` — [packs/product-wiki/README.md](packs/product-wiki/README.md)).

The declaration is **pack-oriented**: a `packs` entry is a pack id, or an entry object carrying that
pack's own settings — its parameters (`config`), its adoption-interview answers (`answers` — a pack
may declare the questions its adoption must ask; the unanswered gap surfaces as a mild SessionStart
note, strict only inside bootstrap), and the rule overrides/acceptances its declaration
motivates (`rules`/`accept`, the entry being their provenance). Top-level `rules`/`accept` stay for
project-wide decisions and skill-owned checks. Full schema: [checks/README.md](checks/README.md).

**Settings validity** — an unknown pack name, an unknown property (top-level or on a pack entry), or
malformed JSON in `.claudinite-checks.json` — is checked when the file loads and surfaced by the runner as
a blocking `config` error (a wrong pack name is as much a settings error as bad JSON), not a conformance
check among the packs. A pack's `marker` only *suspects* the pack is wanted; whether to declare it is the
project's call, so neither a marker without its declaration nor a declaration without its marker is flagged.

A pack that only makes sense alongside another names it in its `requires` list (e.g.
`chrome-extension-release` requires `chrome-extension`, `firebase-release` requires `firebase`,
`spec-driven-product` requires `executable-requirements`). This isn't a check: a pack can't be imported
without its dependencies, so `resolveDeclaredPacks` ([packs/registry.mjs](packs/registry.mjs)) pulls each
declared pack's `requires` closure into the declaration when it's written — at `--init` and the baselining
backfill — materializing the prerequisite in the file, visible like every other entry, as
`{ "id": ..., "via": [...] }` with `via` naming the packs that require it.

## skills/ — activity-scoped procedures, surfaced on demand

The full catalog and each skill's trigger live in [skills/README.md](skills/README.md) — the source of
truth, kept complete against the tree by the `catalog-completeness` check. Two kinds: **command skills**
(owner-phrase or bootstrap triggers — e.g. `merge-to-main`, `lessons-learned`, `bump-version`) and
**practice skills** (surfaced by the activity in front of you — e.g. `bug-investigation`,
`writing-tests`, `engineering-practices`, `file-placement`). See the catalog for the complete list.

## preferences/ — auto-injected by the SessionStart hook

`preferences/<email>.md` holds the owner's per-user interaction preferences. The
`mount/inject-preferences.sh` SessionStart step loads the current user's file into context — you
don't read it yourself.

---

Repo internals — what this repo is, how consumers mount it, the maintenance routines → [README.md](README.md).
Changing the canon without hurting consuming repos → [consumer-safe-changes.md](consumer-safe-changes.md) (provisional).

git add -A && git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
Re-home pack-independence in the canon's own curation pack

The check polices the corpus's packs/ tree — an artifact only the canon home
repo has — so it is a canon constraint, not baseline functionality: no
consumer needs it to use the corpus (a consumer's tree carries vendored packs
the sweep never scans, and the vendor writer's coherence guard holds the same
invariant on their behalf at vendoring time). Move it from the baseline pack
into canon-curation, the home-only pack declared by exactly one repo — this
one — and drop the consumer-local-packs scanning the baseline placement had
justified. The ordering check-the-work test follows the file (git log
--follow keeps the rule's original landing as the anchor commit).

Refs #349

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_01DJX6fa8A293pv5jK4ucAXy
EOF

input.body:
## What broke

Nightly baselining of `GoogleCalendarEventCreator` hit the gated vendored-mount flip and found the vendored tree would be incoherent: `computeVendorSet` omitted `packs/barriers/` while `basics`/`product-wiki` imported `../barriers/engine.mjs`, so the flipped member would crash on the missing import. The worker aborted safely; the bug recurs nightly until fixed (#349).

## The fix — rule first, then the fixes, one commit per phase (by owner direction)

**1. Codify the rule** (`444cb5b`): a pack's code imports only its **own** files and the engine surface (`checks/`, `mount/`, the `packs/`/`skills/` root machinery) — never another pack's code, never a canon-internal tree. Another pack's *abilities* arrive by declaring it (`requires`) and passing configuration. Enforced as the **`pack-independence`** check (blocking, tree-oracle import resolution); `checks/lib/imports.mjs` is the one home for the import-surface facts. Per the ordering meta-rule recorded in `checks/DESIGN.md` — a rule mandating "X before Y" ships a check-the-work verification — the check's test file asserts **on the branch commit log** that the rule commit is an ancestor of each misuse-fix commit. At this commit the check deliberately fired on the four live misuse sites.

**2. Fix the misuse** (`85ab5aa`): the barriers pack gains the **contribution route** — a pack declares `requires: ['barriers']` and carries a fixed barrier as manifest **data** (`contributes: { barriers: [...] }`); `contributed.mjs` builds each into a first-class rule under the contribution's own id, so existing accepts/overrides for `claudinite-isolation` and `product-wiki-isolation` keep working (`gateDir` carries the vendored-mount gate declaratively; `defineBarrier` is gone). `basics`/`product-wiki` contribute their walls as data; `under`/`normPrefix` move to `checks/lib/paths.mjs`; the sync migration surface (`migrationActive`) moves to `checks/lib/migrations.mjs` (closing the latent `chrome-extension-release → migrations/registry.mjs` escape — in a vendored consumer the absent tree answers "no active migrations", correct for a flipped member). `checks/run.mjs` gains the generic `contributedRules` seam; adoption interviews skip `via`-materialized dependencies until the project engages with them.

**3. Migration fix** (`d2c0a39`): the vendor set is coherent by construction now (the requires closure pulls barriers in — this alone unblocks the GCEC flip), and `computeVendorSet` **verifies** it: every vendored module's relative imports must resolve within the set, judged against the same engine-surface definitions the check enforces; a violation aborts convergence before any write, naming the offending import. Regression test pinned to the failure: the `basics` and `product-wiki` vendor sets carry the barriers pack with zero errors. A dated migration note carries the one-time consumer guidance (local packs that composed on the removed export convert to `contributes`).

**4. Placement correction** (`59fa080`): pack-independence polices the corpus's `packs/` tree — an artifact only the canon home has — so it moved from the baseline pack into **canon-curation**, the home-only pack declared by exactly this repo; consumers never run it (the vendor writer's coherence guard holds the invariant on their behalf). The consumer-local-packs scanning the baseline placement had justified is dropped.

## Known one-cycle window

A consumer whose declaration predates the backfill has no `barriers` entry yet, so the contributed walls sit dormant there until its nightly baselining materializes the requires closure — the standard canon-change exposure (mount/DESIGN.md, trade-off 10), self-healing.

## Verification

- 358 tests green (new: check unit tests, contribution factory + gateDir + malformed-contribution, runner seam end-to-end, interview via-gating, vendor coherence guard + real-corpus regression).
- The check-the-work ordering test asserts for real on this branch — rule commit ≺ fix commits — and tracks the rule file across its relocation (`git log --follow`).
- `checks/run.mjs` exits clean; at phase 1 it demonstrably fired on all four outlawed sites (see-it-fail).
- All-packs vendor audit: every declarable pack's set import-closed, zero errors.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

- **`pack-independence` now lives in `packs/canon-curation/`** — the existing home-only pack ("declared by hand in exactly one repo, the canon home itself"), which was precisely built for canon-repo duties. Basics no longer carries it.
- **Simplified to canon scope**: the `.claudinite/local_packs/` and vendored-shared-prefix handling is gone — the check scans `packs/<id>/` only. Consumer-side, the invariant is still held where it matters: the vendor writer's coherence guard (`computeVendorSet`) refuses to ship an import-incoherent set, and a local pack importing the removed export fails loudly at pack discovery. 1	# Claudinite — corpus index
2	
3	> ℹ️ The owner's personal preferences and the active packs' prose are both injected automatically by
4	> SessionStart hooks — they're already in context above. Honor them; there is nothing to go read.
5	
6	**Routing map, not a payload.** Two homes hold the corpus, selected by *when* a rule is active:
7	
8	- **`packs/<name>/`** — a bundle of prose (a pack's `RULES.md`) **and** checks, selected **once per
9	  session** by the project's declaration in `.claudinite-checks.json`. No pack is active by default —
10	  `basics` too is declared explicitly (bootstrap seeds it). The active packs' prose loads at session
11	  start via `packs/load-active-prose.mjs`; the checks run at every Stop and in CI. This is the "decided
12	  once, seldom changes" set. A pack can live here (the shared, portable canon) **or** in a consumer's
13	  own `.claudinite/local_packs/<name>/` (tracked project content — its project-specific packs, the same
14	  slots); the engine discovers and runs both the same way ([extending.md](extending.md)).
15	- **`skills/<name>/`** — activity-scoped procedures, surfaced **on demand** by the harness when the work
16	  in front of you matches the skill. Deployment is pack-driven: each pack declares the skills it
17	  requires, and a repo mounts the union over its active packs at session start
18	  (`skills/mount-skills.mjs`); the `skill-ownership` check keeps every skill required by some pack.
19	  Catalog: [skills/README.md](skills/README.md).
20	
21	Everything enforceable is a check or a hook; everything always-relevant-to-a-project is pack prose;
22	everything activity-scoped is a skill. Before adding *any* rule as prose, run the promotion ladder in
23	[checks/DESIGN.md](checks/DESIGN.md): a platform setting, a hook, a check, or a skill that can carry it
24	beats prose. And before adding *any* feature — a rule, a technology's conventions, a nightly task —
25	decide the core/pack boundary first: [extending.md](extending.md) is the map of what's engine and what's
26	pack-contributed content, and where each kind of feature goes (almost never core).
27	
28	## packs/ — prose + checks, active only when declared (`basics` too)
29	
30	- **[packs/basics/](packs/basics/RULES.md)** — the baseline: working discipline, the task
31	  lifecycle, and the core checks. Declared explicitly like every other pack — bootstrap seeds
32	  the declaration, the nightly baselining backfills it into existing consumers.
33	- **[packs/barriers/](packs/barriers/README.md)** — a mechanism pack: enforce a directed
34	  folder-access graph (folder A may not reference folder B — imports, path/filename references, in
35	<<<<<<< HEAD
36	  any language, comments and docs included), declared per-project as `config` on the repo's barriers pack entry. Its
37	  detection engine is exported so other packs compose their own separation rules on it (the way
38	  `spec-driven-product` builds on `executable-requirements`).
39	- **[packs/grow_with_claudinite/](packs/grow_with_claudinite/README.md)** — the growth
40	  lifecycle's member side (extract/dedup/pack-discovery daily tasks; `config.promote: false`
41	  opts a repo out of the central promote stage) **and the conversation lifecycle**: each merge
42	  to main captures the session's conversation onto an orphan `conversation-logs` branch
43	  (delta-aware when one session merges repeatedly); a **per-repo nightly** (not a fleet task)
44	  extracts lessons from the logs with a rethink window, posts the dialogue behind each
45	  extracted rule on the issue it was worked under, and deletes logs past the declared
46	  `retention_days` — the successor of the retired in-session post-merge lessons pass.

54	- **Technology packs**, active when the project declares them (bootstrap's `--init` seeds the

70	  `product-wiki` (the self-growing product research wiki standard — layout, section/citation/log

73	
74	The declaration is **pack-oriented**: a `packs` entry is a pack id, or an entry object carrying that
75	pack's own settings — its parameters (`config`), its adoption-interview answers (`answers` — a pack
76	may declare the questions its adoption must ask; the unanswered gap surfaces as a mild SessionStart
77	note, strict only inside bootstrap), and the rule overrides/acceptances its declaration
78	motivates (`rules`/`accept`, the entry being their provenance). Top-level `rules`/`accept` stay for
79	project-wide decisions and skill-owned checks. Full schema: [checks/README.md](checks/README.md).
80	
81	**Settings validity** — an unknown pack name, an unknown property (top-level or on a pack entry), or
82	malformed JSON in `.claudinite-checks.json` — is checked when the file loads and surfaced by the runner as
83	a blocking `config` error (a wrong pack name is as much a settings error as bad JSON), not a conformance
84	check among the packs. A pack's `marker` only *suspects* the pack is wanted; whether to declare it is the
85	project's call, so neither a marker without its declaration nor a declaration without its marker is flagged.
86	
87	A pack that only makes sense alongside another names it in its `requires` list (e.g.
88	`chrome-extension-release` requires `chrome-extension`, `firebase-release` requires `firebase`,
89	`spec-driven-product` requires `executable-requirements`). This isn't a check: a pack can't be imported
90	without its dependencies, so `resolveDeclaredPacks` ([packs/registry.mjs](packs/registry.mjs)) pulls each
91	declared pack's `requires` closure into the declaration when it's written — at `--init` and the baselining
92	backfill — materializing the prerequisite in the file, visible like every other entry, as
93	`{ "id": ..., "via": [...] }` with `via` naming the packs that require it.
94

96	
97	The full catalog and each skill's trigger live in [skills/README.md](skills/README.md) — the source of
