attachment.snippet:
1	# The vendored mount — persist Claudinite in each consumer, update nightly (design)
2	
3	> **Status: agreed direction (issue #315); phase 0 implemented, phase 1 in progress.** This doc is
4	> the decision record for replacing the session-start fetch with a tracked, nightly-updated
5	> vendor of the corpus. The live mount ([README.md](README.md)) keeps working unchanged until the
6	> transition phases land.
7	
8	## The problem
9	
10	Both mount methods re-fetch the corpus at every session start — Method B pulls the latest-`main`
11	tarball, Method A updates the submodule. That makes session start network-dependent and fragile:
12	the fetch halt-gate, the `codeload.github.com` allowlist requirement, the `.claudinite.new` swap,
13	and the tracked-copy preservation dance in the sync hook all exist only to manage fetch failure.
14	The freshness the fetch buys is not needed: the core rarely changes, packs change roughly
15	nightly, and nothing requires immediate propagation. So the per-session fetch pays a reliability
16	cost every session for a latency guarantee nothing uses.
17	
18	## The model
19	
20	**A consumer holds a vendored, tracked corpus at `.claudinite/shared/`** — ordinary committed
21	files at canon-relative paths, the same move issue #276 already made for the release workflows,
22	applied to the whole corpus. The **nightly maintenance is the only regular writer**.
23	
24	1. **The `shared/` root is a submodule emulation.** The set materializes under
25	   `.claudinite/shared/` mirroring the canon layout exactly, so the planned future — mounting
26	   Claudinite as a **git submodule** at that same path, once sessions run where a cross-repo git
27	   credential exists — is a drop-in upgrade: the submodule lands a superset at the same root and
28	   **no wiring path changes** (`@.claudinite/shared/CLAUDE.md`,
29	   `node .claudinite/shared/checks/stop-hook.mjs`, …). This is also why nothing consumer-owned
30	   lives inside it: `.claudinite/local_packs/` sits *beside* `shared/`, which the submodule
31	   future outright requires — a submodule directory cannot carry the consumer's files.
32	2. **Minimal set, derived structurally from the declaration.** The vendor set is a function of
33	   `.claudinite-checks.json`, computed by [vendor.mjs](vendor.mjs) with **no hand-maintained
34	   file list**: the engine roots (`checks/`, `mount/`) vendored wholesale, the machinery `.mjs`
35	   files at the `packs/`/`skills/` roots, the **declared packs** with their `requires` closure,
36	   the **union of those packs' skills**, and the corpus index (`CLAUDE.md`). Excluded
37	   everywhere: tests; excluded at the engine roots: `*.md` (canon-maintainer docs are read
38	   upstream when needed — a pack's or skill's `.md` files are the payload and ride their
39	   directories). Canon-internal trees (`routines/`, `docs/`, `.github/`, root maintainer docs)
40	   are never vendored. Two accepted edges: a newly declared pack has no local content until the
41	   next refresh (the engine's unknown-pack `config` error surfaces a declared-but-absent pack
42	   loudly), and links from vendored docs to non-vendored canon files may dangle locally by
43	   design (the sweep never inspects the shared mount — see 6).
44	3. **Preferences are never vendored** — per-user settings, not project content. The
45	   session-start step ([inject-preferences.sh](inject-preferences.sh), mount machinery now)
46	   reads the local `preferences/<email>.md` when its tree carries one (the canon repo; a future
47	   submodule mount; the interim full-tarball sync) and otherwise **fetches just that file** over
48	   HTTPS, fresh each session. Every miss — no email, no file, fetch failure — is **fail-soft**:
49	   a one-line note and the session proceeds on defaults. The halt-gate is reserved for the
50	   load-bearing corpus, which after the flip is always local and can't miss.
51	4. **Transactional nightly update.** Per repo and per night: apply the pending migration notes,
52	   converge the vendored tree to the canon snapshot, advance the stamp — **one commit**. (One
53	   MCP reality: file *deletions* can't ride a `push_files` commit, so convergence's prunes
54	   follow as their own `delete_file` commits **after** the stamped content commit — safe to
55	   interrupt, since the next night's convergence re-deletes stragglers; note ops are
56	   stamp-gated and always land with the stamp — #329.) If the
57	   migration fails, nothing is written: the repo keeps running its old snapshot exactly as
58	   before, is retried the next night, and the failure lands in the fleet routine's failure log.
59	   The commit honors the repo's `maintenance.delivery` (`push` or `pr`; the pr lane computes its
60	   writes against the *default* branch, drops what the maintenance branch already carries, and
61	   refreshes that branch from base each night — regenerate, never reconcile — #332). The refresh is
62	   **unconditional convergence** (copy-if-different, not copy-if-canon-moved), so an accidental
63	   local edit to a vendored file reverts within a day, visibly in the nightly's diff — that,
64	   plus a one-line "canon-owned; propose changes upstream" note, is the whole anti-drift story;
65	   there is deliberately no manifest/integrity framework. Direction-blindness cuts both ways,
66	   so the writer carries the one **anti-rewind guard** the no-framework stance leaves room for:
67	   converging is refused when the checkout's HEAD mismatches the passed ref or the member's
68	   stamped ref is not its ancestor, and the worker verifies the checkout is at the **remote**
69	   head before converging — a stale maintenance checkout must fail its unit, never silently
70	   downgrade the fleet (#328).
71	5. **The stamp.** `"claudinite": { "updated": "<full ISO datetime>", "ref": "<sha>" }` in
72	   `.claudinite-checks.json` — `updated` selects which migration notes still apply (notes are
73	   day-dated, so selection is by the stamp's **day, same-day inclusive** — a note landing later
74	   on the stamp's day must still apply, and note idempotency absorbs the re-application this
75	   admits — #330), `ref` is provenance for debugging **and** the anti-rewind guard's anchor (4). **One stamp for the whole set, never per pack**: updates are
76	   whole-set atomic, because mixed per-pack versions inside one repo would recreate exactly the
77	   engine↔pack skew this design eliminates (declaring a new pack therefore triggers a whole-set
78	   refresh, not a lone pack copy). The engine already tolerates the key
79	   (`CONFIG_KEYS` in checks/lib).
80	6. **The shared mount is structurally out of the sweep.** The engine's file-set builder drops
81	   everything under `.claudinite/shared/` (`buildContext` in checks/lib) — the corpus is
82	   canon-owned, never the project's own code — while `.claudinite/local_packs/` stays fully in
83	   scope. Deliberately a structural rule in the engine, **not** a `.gitattributes` /
84	   `linguist-vendored` convention: the exclusion must hold on any git host and any checkout.
85	   This is also what keeps the corpus-shape checks (catalog-completeness, skill-ownership)
86	   naturally inert in consumers with a pruned tree.
87	7. **Pinning semantics.** Each branch runs the snapshot committed on it: a session, its Stop
88	   hook, and CI all judge by the same law, and a canon change affects no consumer until that
89	   consumer's own nightly commit. Consumer CI becomes a three-line workflow running
90	   `node .claudinite/shared/checks/run.mjs` from the checkout — the backstop
91	   [checks/README.md](../checks/README.md) names finally has standard wiring. Rollback is
92	   per-repo and atomic: revert the nightly's commit.
93	8. **Isolation.** Consumer files must not reference `.claudinite/` except the wiring set: the
94	   root `CLAUDE.md` (the `@`-import and self-check), `.claude/` (settings hook registrations),
95	   `.gitignore`, `.gitattributes`, `.github/workflows/` (the CI stub), and anything under
96	   `.claudinite/` itself (`local_packs/` included). Product code that wants a canon helper

1	# Extending Claudinite — the core/pack boundary and where a feature goes
2	
3	Before adding *anything* to this repo, decide which of two layers it belongs to. Getting this
4	wrong is the recurring failure mode — a rule, a technology's conventions, or a nightly task
5	wired straight into the engine instead of contributed as pack content, where every consumer then
6	pays for it whether or not they need it.
7	
8	## The one principle
9	
10	> **Core is the engine that runs pack-contributed content. A feature is core only if it *is* the
11	> engine.** Everything a project could plausibly not need — a rule, a technology's gotchas, a
12	> maintenance task, a project-class playbook — is pack content, discovered structurally and
13	> activated by declaration. If you can't say "this is machinery every pack relies on," it isn't
14	> core.
15	
16	This is the same instinct the corpus already applies to itself: packs and skills are discovered
17	by *scanning the tree*, not by a hand-maintained list ([packs/registry.mjs](packs/registry.mjs),
18	[skills/registry.mjs](skills/registry.mjs)); the mechanism is fixed, the content is open. New
19	content drops in a directory; new mechanism is a deliberate change to the engine.
20	
21	## What is core (the engine)
22	
23	Core is the small, closed set of machinery that runs, discovers, and schedules content. Touch it
24	only to extend the *mechanism*, never to add one project's rule or task:
25	
26	| Engine piece | Home | What it is |
27	|---|---|---|
28	| Checks runner + hooks | [`checks/`](checks/README.md) | the dependency-free runner, its lib, the Stop hook, the PreToolUse guard — runs the packs' checks; owns no rule itself |
29	| Pack discovery + prose injection | `packs/registry.mjs`, `packs/load-active-prose.mjs` | structural scan of `packs/*/pack.mjs`; SessionStart injection of active packs' prose |
30	| Skill discovery + mounting | `skills/registry.mjs`, `skills/mount-skills.mjs` | structural scan; per-session symlink of the active packs' skill union |
31	| Adoption interviews | `packs/interview.mjs` | the gap computation (a pack's declared questions minus the entry's stored answers) and the SessionStart nudge; owns no question itself |
32	| Baseline-migration mechanism | [`migrations/`](migrations/README.md) | the read-side resolver, write-side rename, and fleet telemetry that auto-retires a relocation once every consumer has moved |
33	| The run_daily planner | [`routines/fleet/`](routines/fleet/DESIGN.md) | goes over the reachable repos, assembles each one's due tasks from its active packs, masks full-sweep, isolates a throwing gate, emits the plan; pack-agnostic, owns no task, depends on no pack |
34	| The orchestrator | [`routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md`](routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md) | the single scheduled entry point — runs the planner over the accessible fleet, reads the plan, fans out the units |
35	| Bootstrap / baselining | [`bootstrap.md`](bootstrap.md), `checks/run.mjs --init` | adoption and the idempotent per-repo re-run |
36	
37	**The test for "is this core?"** — would *every* pack's content stop working without it? The
38	planner, the runner, the migration mechanism, the orchestrator loop all pass; a lint for one
39	technology, a nightly release task, a naming rule all fail. Two responsibilities are core *by
40	ownership* even though they run as pack tasks: **baselining** (the baseline pack's daily task) and the
41	**daily-run** itself are Claudinite's job, not a pack's — the pack is only the delivery slot.
42	
43	## What a pack contributes
44	
45	A pack is a directory `packs/<name>/pack.mjs` exporting contribution slots (any subset
46	— a pack may carry only prose, only a task, only checks):
47	
48	| Slot | Field | Carries |
49	|---|---|---|
50	| **Prose** | `prose: 'RULES.md'` | always-relevant-to-a-project guidance, injected into context when the pack is active |
51	| **Checks** | `rules: [...]` | deterministic conformance rules run at every Stop and in CI |
52	| **Skills** | `skills: [...]` | activity-scoped procedures mounted wherever the pack is declared |
53	| **Daily tasks** | `run_daily: [...]` | `(gate, worker)` maintenance units the planner picks up — each declares `full_sweep_supported` and its `smarts` tier |
54	| **Questions** | `questions: [...]` | mandatory adoption-interview questions; the owner's answers live verbatim on the project's pack entry ([packs/README.md](packs/README.md#adoption-interview-questions)) |
55	| **Contributed config** | `contributes: { <pack>: ... }` | configuration addressed to another (required) pack — a fixed folder-barrier is the canonical case. The target pack interprets its active contributors' data via its own `contributedRules(activePacks)` seam, returning first-class rules; the runner wires the two together, so composition is declaration + data, never a cross-pack import |
56	
57	**Packs are independent.** A pack's code imports only its **own** files and the engine surface
58	(`checks/`, `mount/`, the machinery `.mjs` at the `packs/`/`skills/` roots) — never another
59	pack's code, and never a canon-internal tree (`migrations/`, `routines/`): the vendor set ships
60	a pack only when declared and ships no canon-internal tree at all, so such an import crashes
61	every consumer that vendors the importer without its target. A pack that wants another pack's
62	*abilities* declares the dependency (`requires`) and passes **configuration**; a helper both
63	sides need moves into `checks/lib`. Enforced by the baseline `pack-independence` check.
64	
65	Activation is the project's declaration in `.claudinite-checks.json` — **no pack runs undeclared,
66	the baseline included.** A technology pack carries a `detect` fingerprint so `--init` seeds it into a
67	fresh declaration when the technology is present; the marker only *suspects* a pack is wanted,
68	never forcing or forbidding its declaration afterward. A declared-by-policy pack (the baseline and
69	the default-on maintenance packs) sets `detect: null` and is seeded by `--init`
70	and/or a one-time migration.
71	
72	### Two homes for a pack: the canon, and a project's own `local_packs/`
73	
74	A pack contributes the same slots from either of two homes, and the engine runs both the
75	same way:
76	
77	- **A canon pack** — `packs/<name>/` in this repo, mounted read-only into every consumer. It is
78	  *portable*: written as if no one project existed, shared by every project that declares it.
79	- **A local pack** — `.claudinite/local_packs/<name>/` in a **consumer's own tree**, tracked
80	  project content the consumer authors and commits. It is *project-specific*: the working style,
81	  values, checks, and skills that don't generalize past this one repo — the project's
82	  **normalized capture surface** (what used to sprawl as always-loaded `CLAUDE.md`/`dev/procedures`
83	  prose). `discoverPacks` scans both roots; a local pack carries its own `dir`, is `local: true`,
84	  and may not shadow a canon id. Prose injection, the Stop/CI checks, and skill mounting treat a
85	  declared local pack exactly like a canon one. (A local pack may also declare `run_daily` tasks, and
86	  the fleet planner has a tested seam for them, but that daily-run path is **experimental and not
87	  enabled by default** — see [packs/README.md](packs/README.md); scheduled work stays a canon-pack
88	  task or an out-of-repo routine for now.)
89	
90	The split is the same **portable-vs-specific** line the growth lifecycle already draws: a rule true
91	beyond this project belongs in a canon pack (proposed by PR, or promoted up by the growth routine);
92	a rule specific to this project belongs in its local pack. Neither is ever "hardcode it into the
93	engine."
94	
95	## Where a new feature goes — the routing
96	
97	Ask what *kind* of thing you're adding; each kind has exactly one home, and none of them is core:
98	
99	1. **A new rule or practice** → the [promotion ladder](checks/DESIGN.md) (platform setting →
100	   PreToolUse hook → post-hoc check → skill → prose) picks the mechanism; it lands in a pack (or

# Claudinite — corpus index

> ℹ️ The owner's personal preferences and the active packs' prose are both injected automatically by
> SessionStart hooks — they're already in context above. Honor them; there is nothing to go read.

**Routing map, not a payload.** Two homes hold the corpus, selected by *when* a rule is active:

- **`packs/<name>/`** — a bundle of prose (a pack's `RULES.md`) **and** checks, selected **once per
  session** by the project's declaration in `.claudinite-checks.json`. No pack is active by default —
  `basics` too is declared explicitly (bootstrap seeds it). The active packs' prose loads at session
  start via `packs/load-active-prose.mjs`; the checks run at every Stop and in CI. This is the "decided
  once, seldom changes" set. A pack can live here (the shared, portable canon) **or** in a consumer's
  own `.claudinite/local_packs/<name>/` (tracked project content — its project-specific packs, the same
  slots); the engine discovers and runs both the same way ([extending.md](extending.md)).
- **`skills/<name>/`** — activity-scoped procedures, surfaced **on demand** by the harness when the work
  in front of you matches the skill. Deployment is pack-driven: each pack declares the skills it
  requires, and a repo mounts the union over its active packs at session start
  (`skills/mount-skills.mjs`); the `skill-ownership` check keeps every skill required by some pack.
  Catalog: [skills/README.md](skills/README.md).

Everything enforceable is a check or a hook; everything always-relevant-to-a-project is pack prose;
everything activity-scoped is a skill. Before adding *any* rule as prose, run the promotion ladder in
[checks/DESIGN.md](checks/DESIGN.md): a platform setting, a hook, a check, or a skill that can carry it
beats prose. And before adding *any* feature — a rule, a technology's conventions, a nightly task —
decide the core/pack boundary first: [extending.md](extending.md) is the map of what's engine and what's
pack-contributed content, and where each kind of feature goes (almost never core).

## packs/ — prose + checks, active only when declared (`basics` too)

- **[packs/basics/](packs/basics/RULES.md)** — the baseline: working discipline, the task
  lifecycle, and the core checks. Declared explicitly like every other pack — bootstrap seeds
  the declaration, the nightly baselining backfills it into existing consumers.
- **[packs/barriers/](packs/barriers/README.md)** — a mechanism pack: enforce a directed
  folder-access graph (folder A may not reference folder B — imports, path/filename references, in
<<<<<<< HEAD
  any language, comments and docs included), declared per-project as `config` on the repo's barriers pack entry. Its
  detection engine is exported so other packs compose their own separation rules on it (the way
  `spec-driven-product` builds on `executable-requirements`).
- **[packs/grow_with_claudinite/](packs/grow_with_claudinite/README.md)** — the growth
  lifecycle's member side (extract/dedup/pack-discovery daily tasks; `config.promote: false`
  opts a repo out of the central promote stage) **and the conversation lifecycle**: each merge
  to main captures the session's conversation onto an orphan `conversation-logs` branch
  (delta-aware when one session merges repeatedly); a **per-repo nightly** (not a fleet task)
  extracts lessons from the logs with a rethink window, posts the dialogue behind each
  extracted rule on the issue it was worked under, and deletes logs past the declared
  `retention_days` — the successor of the retired in-session post-merge lessons pass.
=======

  Other packs compose their own separation rules on it by *declaring* it (`requires`) and
  *contributing* a fixed barrier as manifest data (`contributes` — the way `basics` and
  `product-wiki` ship their isolation walls); no pack imports another's code (the baseline
  `pack-independence` check).
>>>>>>> 85ab5aa (Fix the cross-pack misuse: barriers composed by contribution, helpers to the engine lib)
- **Technology packs**, active when the project declares them (bootstrap's `--init` seeds the
  declaration from a fingerprint): `chrome-extension` (MV3 coding gotchas, fingerprinted by the
  manifest), `chrome-extension-release` (the *opt-in* release/store standard in its
  [RELEASE.md](packs/chrome-extension-release/RELEASE.md) + the conformance checks — declared when the
  project is ready to ship, fingerprinted by its single `Release to Chrome Store` workflow stub), `github-actions`
  (workflow lints), `node`, `aws-sam`, `html`, `flutter`, `firebase` (rules/functions/deploy
  discipline, fingerprinted by `firebase.json`) with its opt-in `firebase-release` (dev/prod
  project split + App Check store gating — declared near shipping), and the stubs `android`,
  `ios`, `play-store-release`, `app-store-release` (filled when first exercised).
- **Project-class packs**, declared by kind of project: `research-project` (the
  algorithm-iteration playbook — [packs/research-project/RULES.md](packs/research-project/RULES.md))
  and `spec-driven-product` (the executable-spec product playbook —
  [packs/spec-driven-product/RULES.md](packs/spec-driven-product/RULES.md)) with its mechanics
  companion `executable-requirements` (the framework standard — layout, gates, kinds incl. the
  storyboard `saga` kind, gallery, determinism; fingerprinted by `dev/requirements/requirements.md`
  — [packs/executable-requirements/RULES.md](packs/executable-requirements/RULES.md)); and
  `product-wiki` (the self-growing product research wiki standard — layout, section/citation/log
  checks, a fixed isolation barrier, a weekly growth daily task; fingerprinted by
  `product-wiki/product-requirements/README.md` — [packs/product-wiki/README.md](packs/product-wiki/README.md)).

The declaration is **pack-oriented**: a `packs` entry is a pack id, or an entry object carrying that
pack's own settings — its parameters (`config`), its adoption-interview answers (`answers` — a pack
may declare the questions its adoption must ask; the unanswered gap surfaces as a mild SessionStart
note, strict only inside bootstrap), and the rule overrides/acceptances its declaration
motivates (`rules`/`accept`, the entry being their provenance). Top-level `rules`/`accept` stay for
project-wide decisions and skill-owned checks. Full schema: [checks/README.md](checks/README.md).

**Settings validity** — an unknown pack name, an unknown property (top-level or on a pack entry), or
malformed JSON in `.claudinite-checks.json` — is checked when the file loads and surfaced by the runner as
a blocking `config` error (a wrong pack name is as much a settings error as bad JSON), not a conformance
check among the packs. A pack's `marker` only *suspects* the pack is wanted; whether to declare it is the
project's call, so neither a marker without its declaration nor a declaration without its marker is flagged.

A pack that only makes sense alongside another names it in its `requires` list (e.g.
`chrome-extension-release` requires `chrome-extension`, `firebase-release` requires `firebase`,
`spec-driven-product` requires `executable-requirements`). This isn't a check: a pack can't be imported
without its dependencies, so `resolveDeclaredPacks` ([packs/registry.mjs](packs/registry.mjs)) pulls each
declared pack's `requires` closure into the declaration when it's written — at `--init` and the baselining
backfill — materializing the prerequisite in the file, visible like every other entry, as
`{ "id": ..., "via": [...] }` with `via` naming the packs that require it.

## skills/ — activity-scoped procedures, surfaced on demand

The full catalog and each skill's trigger live in [skills/README.md](skills/README.md) — the source of
truth, kept complete against the tree by the `catalog-completeness` check. Two kinds: **command skills**
(owner-phrase or bootstrap triggers — e.g. `merge-to-main`, `bump-version`) and
**practice skills** (surfaced by the activity in front of you — e.g. `bug-investigation`,
`writing-tests`, `engineering-practices`, `file-placement`). See the catalog for the complete list.

## preferences/ — auto-injected by the SessionStart hook

# Claudinite — corpus index

> ℹ️ The owner's personal preferences and the active packs' prose are both injected automatically by
> SessionStart hooks — they're already in context above. Honor them; there is nothing to go read.

**Routing map, not a payload.** Two homes hold the corpus, selected by *when* a rule is active:

- **`packs/<name>/`** — a bundle of prose (a pack's `RULES.md`) **and** checks, selected **once per
  session** by the project's declaration in `.claudinite-checks.json`. No pack is active by default —
  `basics` too is declared explicitly (bootstrap seeds it). The active packs' prose loads at session
  start via `packs/load-active-prose.mjs`; the checks run at every Stop and in CI. This is the "decided
  once, seldom changes" set. A pack can live here (the shared, portable canon) **or** in a consumer's
  own `.claudinite/local_packs/<name>/` (tracked project content — its project-specific packs, the same
  slots); the engine discovers and runs both the same way ([extending.md](extending.md)).
- **`skills/<name>/`** — activity-scoped procedures, surfaced **on demand** by the harness when the work
  in front of you matches the skill. Deployment is pack-driven: each pack declares the skills it
  requires, and a repo mounts the union over its active packs at session start
  (`skills/mount-skills.mjs`); the `skill-ownership` check keeps every skill required by some pack.
  Catalog: [skills/README.md](skills/README.md).

Everything enforceable is a check or a hook; everything always-relevant-to-a-project is pack prose;
everything activity-scoped is a skill. Before adding *any* rule as prose, run the promotion ladder in
[checks/DESIGN.md](checks/DESIGN.md): a platform setting, a hook, a check, or a skill that can carry it
beats prose. And before adding *any* feature — a rule, a technology's conventions, a nightly task —
decide the core/pack boundary first: [extending.md](extending.md) is the map of what's engine and what's
pack-contributed content, and where each kind of feature goes (almost never core).

## packs/ — prose + checks, active only when declared (`basics` too)

- **[packs/basics/](packs/basics/RULES.md)** — the baseline: working discipline, the task
  lifecycle, and the core checks. Declared explicitly like every other pack — bootstrap seeds
  the declaration, the nightly baselining backfills it into existing consumers.
- **[packs/barriers/](packs/barriers/README.md)** — a mechanism pack: enforce a directed
  folder-access graph (folder A may not reference folder B — imports, path/filename references, in
  any language, comments and docs included), declared per-project as `config` on the repo's barriers pack entry.
  Other packs compose their own separation rules on it by *declaring* it (`requires`) and
  *contributing* a fixed barrier as manifest data (`contributes` — the way `basics` and

- **Technology packs**, active when the project declares them (bootstrap's `--init` seeds the
  declaration from a fingerprint): `chrome-extension` (MV3 coding gotchas, fingerprinted by the
  manifest), `chrome-extension-release` (the *opt-in* release/store standard in its
  [RELEASE.md](packs/chrome-extension-release/RELEASE.md) + the conformance checks — declared when the
  project is ready to ship, fingerprinted by its single `Release to Chrome Store` workflow stub), `github-actions`
  (workflow lints), `node`, `aws-sam`, `html`, `flutter`, `firebase` (rules/functions/deploy
  discipline, fingerprinted by `firebase.json`) with its opt-in `firebase-release` (dev/prod
  project split + App Check store gating — declared near shipping), and the stubs `android`,
  `ios`, `play-store-release`, `app-store-release` (filled when first exercised).
- **Project-class packs**, declared by kind of project: `research-project` (the
  algorithm-iteration playbook — [packs/research-project/RULES.md](packs/research-project/RULES.md))
  and `spec-driven-product` (the executable-spec product playbook —
  [packs/spec-driven-product/RULES.md](packs/spec-driven-product/RULES.md)) with its mechanics
  companion `executable-requirements` (the framework standard — layout, gates, kinds incl. the
  storyboard `saga` kind, gallery, determinism; fingerprinted by `dev/requirements/requirements.md`
  — [packs/executable-requirements/RULES.md](packs/executable-requirements/RULES.md)); and
  `product-wiki` (the self-growing product research wiki standard — layout, section/citation/log
  checks, a fixed isolation barrier, a weekly growth daily task; fingerprinted by
  `product-wiki/product-requirements/README.md` — [packs/product-wiki/README.md](packs/product-wiki/README.md)).

The declaration is **pack-oriented**: a `packs` entry is a pack id, or an entry object carrying that
pack's own settings — its parameters (`config`), its adoption-interview answers (`answers` — a pack
may declare the questions its adoption must ask; the unanswered gap surfaces as a mild SessionStart
note, strict only inside bootstrap), and the rule overrides/acceptances its declaration
motivates (`rules`/`accept`, the entry being their provenance). Top-level `rules`/`accept` stay for
project-wide decisions and skill-owned checks. Full schema: [checks/README.md](checks/README.md).

**Settings validity** — an unknown pack name, an unknown property (top-level or on a pack entry), or
malformed JSON in `.claudinite-checks.json` — is checked when the file loads and surfaced by the runner as
a blocking `config` error (a wrong pack name is as much a settings error as bad JSON), not a conformance
check among the packs. A pack's `marker` only *suspects* the pack is wanted; whether to declare it is the
project's call, so neither a marker without its declaration nor a declaration without its marker is flagged.

A pack that only makes sense alongside another names it in its `requires` list (e.g.
`chrome-extension-release` requires `chrome-extension`, `firebase-release` requires `firebase`,
`spec-driven-product` requires `executable-requirements`). This isn't a check: a pack can't be imported
without its dependencies, so `resolveDeclaredPacks` ([packs/registry.mjs](packs/registry.mjs)) pulls each
declared pack's `requires` closure into the declaration when it's written — at `--init` and the baselining
backfill — materializing the prerequisite in the file, visible like every other entry, as
`{ "id": ..., "via": [...] }` with `via` naming the packs that require it.

## skills/ — activity-scoped procedures, surfaced on demand

The full catalog and each skill's trigger live in [skills/README.md](skills/README.md) — the source of
truth, kept complete against the tree by the `catalog-completeness` check. Two kinds: **command skills**
(owner-phrase or bootstrap triggers — e.g. `merge-to-main`, `bump-version`) and
**practice skills** (surfaced by the activity in front of you — e.g. `bug-investigation`,
`writing-tests`, `engineering-practices`, `file-placement`). See the catalog for the complete list.

## preferences/ — auto-injected by the SessionStart hook

`preferences/<email>.md` holds the owner's per-user interaction preferences. The
`mount/inject-preferences.sh` SessionStart step loads the current user's file into context — you
don't read it yourself.

---

Repo internals — what this repo is, how consumers mount it, the maintenance routines → [README.md](README.md).
Changing the canon without hurting consuming repos → [consumer-safe-changes.md](consumer-safe-changes.md) (provisional).

+  `product-wiki` ship their isolation walls); no pack imports another's code (the
+  `pack-independence` check, a canon-home curation duty).
 - **[packs/grow_with_claudinite/](packs/grow_with_claudinite/README.md)** — the growth
   lifecycle's member side (extract/dedup/pack-discovery daily tasks; `config.promote: false`
   opts a repo out of the central promote stage) **and the conversation lifecycle**: each merge

content.subject: Phase 1: codify pack-independence rule (check + ordering check-the-work test + docs)
content.description: New basics check: a pack's code may import only its own files and the always-vendored engine surface (checks/lib, packs//skills/ root machinery) — never another pack's code or canon-internal trees. Cross-pack abilities flow through declaration + configuration. Includes the check-the-work test verifying (on the branch commit log) that the rule commit precedes the fix commits, per the owner's ordering meta-rule; plus DESIGN/extending doc updates. Own commit.

content.subject: Phase 2: fix the cross-pack misuse via declaration + configuration
content.description: Barriers pack gains contributed-barriers ability (packs contribute fixed barrier rules as data via pack.mjs `contributes`); basics + product-wiki require barriers and contribute their isolation barriers as config, dropping the defineBarrier imports; move `under` to checks/lib; move migrationActive to checks/lib so chrome-extension-release stops importing migrations/registry.mjs; update stale docs. Own commit.

content.subject: Phase 3: relevant migration fix (vendor coherence + consumer note)
content.description: Drop the import-closure approach (rebuild branch from origin/main); vendor set now carries barriers via the requires closure. Add real-corpus regression test (basics/product-wiki vendor sets include packs/barriers, import-closed) and a computeVendorSet coherence validation that errors on escaping imports; dated migration note for consumers whose local packs composed on the removed defineBarrier export. Own commit. Then update PR #348 + issue #349.

content.text:
{"number":348,"title":"Pack independence: no cross-pack code imports — compose by declaration + contributed config (fixes the GCEC vendored-tree abort)","body":"## What broke

Nightly baselining of `GoogleCalendarEventCreator` hit the gated vendored-mount flip and found the vendored tree would be incoherent: `computeVendorSet` omitted `packs/barriers/` while `basics`/`product-wiki` imported `../barriers/engine.mjs`, so the flipped member would crash on the missing import. The worker aborted safely; the bug recurs nightly until fixed (#349).

## The fix — rule first, then the fixes, one commit per phase (by owner direction)

**1. Codify the rule** (`444cb5b`): a pack's code imports only its **own** files and the engine surface (`checks/`, `mount/`, the `packs/`/`skills/` root machinery) — never another pack's code, never a canon-internal tree. Another pack's *abilities* arrive by declaring it (`requires`) and passing configuration. Enforced as the **`pack-independence`** check (blocking, tree-oracle import resolution); `checks/lib/imports.mjs` is the one home for the import-surface facts. Per the ordering meta-rule recorded in `checks/DESIGN.md` — a rule mandating "X before Y" ships a check-the-work verification — the check's test file asserts **on the branch commit log** that the rule commit is an ancestor of each misuse-fix commit. At this commit the check deliberately fired on the four live misuse sites.

**2. Fix the misuse** (`85ab5aa`): the barriers pack gains the **contribution route** — a pack declares `requires: ['barriers']` and carries a fixed barrier as manifest **data** (`contributes: { barriers: [...] }`); `contributed.mjs` builds each into a first-class rule under the contribution's own id, so existing accepts/overrides for `claudinite-isolation` and `product-wiki-isolation` keep working (`gateDir` carries the vendored-mount gate declaratively; `defineBarrier` is gone). `basics`/`product-wiki` contribute their walls as data; `under`/`normPrefix` move to `checks/lib/paths.mjs`; the sync migration surface (`migrationActive`) moves to `checks/lib/migrations.mjs` (closing the latent `chrome-extension-release → migrations/registry.mjs` escape — in a vendored consumer the absent tree answers "no active migrations", correct for a flipped member). `checks/run.mjs` gains the generic `contributedRules` seam; adoption interviews skip `via`-materialized dependencies until the project engages with them.

**3. Migration fix** (`d2c0a39`): the vendor set is coherent by construction now (the requires closure pulls barriers in — this alone unblocks the GCEC flip), and `computeVendorSet` **verifies** it: every vendored module's relative imports must resolve within the set, judged against the same engine-surface definitions the check enforces; a violation aborts convergence before any write, naming the offending import. Regression test pinned to the failure: the `basics` and `product-wiki` vendor sets carry the barriers pack with zero errors. A dated migration note carries the one-time consumer guidance (local packs that composed on the removed export convert to `contributes`).

**4. Placement correction** (`59fa080`): pack-independence polices the corpus's `packs/` tree — an artifact only the canon home has — so it moved from the baseline pack into **canon-curation**, the home-only pack declared by exactly this repo; consumers never run it (the vendor writer's coherence guard holds the invariant on their behalf). The consumer-local-packs scanning the baseline placement had justified is dropped.

## Known one-cycle window

A consumer whose declaration predates the backfill has no `barriers` entry yet, so the contributed walls sit dormant there until its nightly baselining materializes the requires closure — the standard canon-change exposure (mount/DESIGN.md, trade-off 10), self-healing.

## Verification

- 358 tests green (new: check unit tests, contribution factory + gateDir + malformed-contribution, runner seam end-to-end, interview via-gating, vendor coherence guard + real-corpus regression).
- The check-the-work ordering test asserts for real on this branch — rule commit ≺ fix commits — and tracks the rule file across its relocation (`git log --follow`).
- `checks/run.mjs` exits clean; at phase 1 it demonstrably fired on all four outlawed sites (see-it-fail).
- All-packs vendor audit: every declarable pack's set import-closed, zero errors.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

# Owner interaction preferences

Personal preferences from the repo owner for how the assistant should interact — tone, summary style, end-of-turn conventions, how results and decisions are surfaced, and the phrases the owner uses to trigger defined commands. These are **not** project conventions (those live in the [packs/](../packs/) and [skills/](../skills/) corpus); they're how the owner likes to be worked with. Where a preference triggers a command whose mechanics are a project convention, the mechanics stay in their own doc and the entry here just owns the trigger phrase.

## Preferences

- **When there's nothing left to do, say so boldly at the end of the conversation.** Don't bury a "this is complete / I'm blocked / nothing further to do" behind hedging — make the terminal state unmistakable. **Set it apart visually so it catches the eye:** put the terminal state — or, when work remains, the list of open/outstanding items — in its own callout (a Markdown blockquote box, e.g. `> ✅ All done` or `> ⚠️ Still open: …`), not trailing prose the owner has to scan for. One or the other always gets the box: the done-signal when nothing remains, the open-items list when something does.
- **"LGTM"** is the owner's phrase for the merge-to-main command: it asks for the change in front of the owner to be merged into `main`. It's an owner-triggered command; the default mechanics live in the `merge-to-main` skill ([../skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md](../skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md)) (squash, gate on CI only when the repo has it), and a project that diverges overrides them via a bespoke merge-policy file it names in its own `CLAUDE.md`.
- **"bump version"** is the owner's phrase for raising a project's version (a minor bump by default). It's an owner-triggered command; the mechanics — which files to edit and how a release follows — live in the consuming project's workflow doc.

# Owner interaction preferences

Personal preferences from the repo owner for how the assistant should interact — tone, summary style, end-of-turn conventions, how results and decisions are surfaced, and the phrases the owner uses to trigger defined commands. These are **not** project conventions (those live in the [packs/](../packs/) and [skills/](../skills/) corpus); they're how the owner likes to be worked with. Where a preference triggers a command whose mechanics are a project convention, the mechanics stay in their own doc and the entry here just owns the trigger phrase.

## Preferences

- **When there's nothing left to do, say so boldly at the end of the conversation.** Don't bury a "this is complete / I'm blocked / nothing further to do" behind hedging — make the terminal state unmistakable. **Set it apart visually so it catches the eye:** put the terminal state — or, when work remains, the list of open/outstanding items — in its own callout (a Markdown blockquote box, e.g. `> ✅ All done` or `> ⚠️ Still open: …`), not trailing prose the owner has to scan for. One or the other always gets the box: the done-signal when nothing remains, the open-items list when something does.
- **"LGTM"** is the owner's phrase for the merge-to-main command: it asks for the change in front of the owner to be merged into `main`. It's an owner-triggered command; the default mechanics live in the `merge-to-main` skill ([../skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md](../skills/merge-to-main/SKILL.md)) (squash, gate on CI only when the repo has it), and a project that diverges overrides them via a bespoke merge-policy file it names in its own `CLAUDE.md`.
- **"bump version"** is the owner's phrase for raising a project's version (a minor bump by default). It's an owner-triggered command; the mechanics — which files to edit and how a release follows — live in the consuming project's workflow doc.
- **"Show me the document"** (for a source-controlled document) is the owner's phrase asking for a **link to the document on GitHub** — not the file's contents inlined, and not a local filesystem path. When the document lives in a GitHub-hosted repo, respond with a GitHub link to it (the file on the relevant branch/commit); only fall back to inlining the contents or giving a local path when the document isn't under source control.
- **Put decisions and approvals in an `AskUserQuestion` popup, not buried in prose.** The owner is frequently on mobile, where a popup notifies and a question embedded in a paragraph is easy to miss. When you need the owner to choose or approve something, ask through the structured popup with explicit options rather than asking in running text. **And when several independent items each need a verdict** (a set of proposed fixes, findings to accept), give **each item its own question carrying its own brief case** — the concrete failure, why it matters, the cost of the fix — with per-item approve/defer options; never one bundled choose-a-scope question over the whole set. The owner decides item-wise, from the case, not from a menu of subsets. (The popup carries at most 4 questions per call — split a larger set into consecutive calls.)
- **When asked to produce a prompt or message destined for another context, visually enclose the whole thing.** Any text the owner will lift out and use elsewhere — a prompt for another assistant, a message to send, copy meant to be pasted somewhere — must be set apart as a single delimited block: either a box (e.g. a fenced code block) or fenced top and bottom by two separating lines, so its exact boundaries are unmistakable and it can be copied in one piece. The point is delimitation, not formatting flair: never leave such content as loose prose the owner has to find the start and end of.
- **Auto-open a PR after committing.** Whenever Claude changes code on a branch and commits it, open a pull request for that branch right after, even without an explicit request to do so. This overrides Claude Code's own default of only opening a PR when explicitly asked.
# Claudinite — active-pack guidance

The baseline plus the packs this project declares. Deeper per-pack reference (e.g. a pack's release doc) is linked from its prose and read on demand.

<!-- pack:basics -->
# Working discipline

The working discipline that isn't itself a GitHub operation — general habits for how to approach a change, independent of any one project.

Start every requested change from the *problem*, not the solution. When the owner asks for a change, don't begin implementing it until the two of you share an explicit understanding of the problem it's meant to solve **and** have agreed that the requested change is the best way to solve it — a different fix, or no change at all, may serve the underlying problem better. Treat the request as a description of a desired outcome, not a specification to execute on sight; surface your read of the problem, raise a better approach if you see one, and reach consensus first. This applies to any repository, not just this one.

An interactive comment from the owner carries one of three build-relevant intents — or none of them — and the classification decides where the change lands and what must exist before any fix. Open your reply to an owner comment with an explicit classification line — `Comment class: correction | feature | process-change | other` (`other` covers questions, approvals, and command phrases; a mixed comment names each part) — the assessment itself stays judgment.

1. **A correction** — you misunderstood something. Repair the shared understanding, then rework what the misread already touched — the artifact changes as much as the correction demands; what doesn't appear is a new requirement or rule.
2. **A feature** — a feature run: agree on the requirement, record it in the project's requirements document (its executable spec, where it keeps one), write the test that proves it and watch it fail, then implement until it passes.
3. **A process change** — the owner is changing *how* work is done. The change lands as durable rules in the project's local scope — its own local packs (in Claudinite itself, its packs) — routed through the mechanism promotion ladder (platform setting → hook → check → skill → prose); promoting a rule into the shared canon is the growth lifecycle's separate call, not the interactive session's. Once the intent is understood, author the assurance first: the check the future world must satisfy. Execute it and watch it fail — the failure is the evidence it detects exactly what the owner was worried about — and only then make the fixes that turn it green. When the ladder lands the rule at prose (an in-flight judgment rule no check can carry), the same step is demonstrating the gap: show the corpus doesn't already cover the rule before writing it.

The two build modes share one spine: state the expectation in its durable home first, watch it fail against the current world, then change the world to satisfy it. A fix made before its assurance exists can never show it addressed what the owner actually asked for — and an unrouted comment tends to become a one-off patch where a requirement or a rule should have accumulated.

Confirm a behavior isn't already provided before building a mechanism for it — verify the gap against a real run first; the cheapest fix is often that it already works.

When feedback flags a misunderstanding, don't reflexively expand the artifact to compensate. First check whether it's already correct — if it is, say so and push back rather than editing; a misread doesn't imply the text is wrong (the sibling of the rule above). And size writing to its idea: "open one issue" takes a sentence, not three paragraphs — prose padded to preempt confusion usually breeds it.

When correcting or auditing an artifact against an authoritative source, derive the corrected version from the *source* before reading the existing draft — reading the draft first anchors you to its framing and quietly carries its errors into your "fix." Build from the source, then diff against the old draft to surface what was actually wrong; a clean-room rebuild catches biases an edit-in-place pass silently inherits.

Fix build/test/CI warnings, don't tolerate them: a clean run with no warnings makes a genuinely new warning or error stand out, so noise here costs detection later. Prefer a small, targeted fix that addresses the *cause* in the same change.

Suppressing a warning — muting it with a flag (e.g. `--disable-warning`), `eslint-disable`, swallowing it, etc. — is **not** a small fix: it hides the signal instead of resolving it. Never reach for suppression as the quick path — it's only ever a deliberate, reviewed decision, made once the real fix has been weighed and rejected, never an unattended default. A suppression you do keep must **carry its reason at the site**: an explanation on the suppression line, or in the comment immediately above it, saying why the fix was rejected. That inline reason *is* the review record — it's what separates a considered suppression from a reflexive mute, and it's why the suppression needs no second justification recorded elsewhere.

When a warning can't be fixed with a small cause-addressing change now without hindering current work (e.g. it's waiting on an upstream release, or the real fix is a larger refactor), open a dedicated issue for it (unless one is already open) so it's tracked and not lost — then move on. Resolving it (real fix, or a consciously-chosen suppression) happens in that issue's own change.

An approval — to merge, to ship, to proceed — applies only *backward*, to the work already in front of the owner when it's given, never to anything requested or done *after* it. A later follow-up, even a fix to the just-approved change, needs its own explicit approval; don't carry one approval forward, and don't treat a chosen answer to a multiple-choice prompt as authorization just because an option's wording mentioned the action. When in doubt, surface the new state and wait for a fresh approval.

# The task lifecycle

The issue → branch → PR lifecycle every new task follows, independent of any one project; this section keeps the method. The rest of the git/GitHub procedures live in the `git-github-advanced` skill.

For every new task:

1. Create a GitHub issue describing the task before starting work.
2. Develop on a branch; reference that issue number in commit messages (e.g. `Refs #123`, `Fixes #123`, or `Closes #123`).
3. Update the issue's status (comments / close) as work progresses and when it's done.

content.text: "LGTM" — merging PR #348 into `main`. Let me follow the merge-to-main skill.

Base directory for this skill: /home/user/Claudinite/.claude/skills/merge-to-main

The portable recipe for landing the change in front of the owner on `main` — the *mechanics* behind the owner's merge-to-main command (the owner's preferences own the trigger *phrase*; this file owns the *how*). Invoked through the `merge-to-main` skill (this skill); this doc stays the canonical recipe.

**A project's bespoke merge policy overrides this default.** Most projects need nothing here — this default is the whole story. A project that genuinely diverges (a different merge method, a CI/twice-green gate, an extra approval) **states so by naming its merge-policy file explicitly in its own `CLAUDE.md`**. When that declaration is present, read that file first and let it override the divergent points below; when it's absent, follow this default as-is. Don't go hunting for a policy file that the project's `CLAUDE.md` doesn't name.

## Recipe (~4 calls)

1. Load both GitHub tools in **one** `ToolSearch`: `create_pull_request` + `merge_pull_request`.
2. No PR open for the branch yet? `create_pull_request` (base `main`); end the body with `Closes #<issue>`, and take the PR number from the returned URL.
3. Gate on CI **only if the repo has it** — a workflow that actually runs on PRs/pushes. `workflow_call`-only reusable workflows (this repo hosts some) are *not* CI: they never run here, so a non-empty `.github/workflows/` alone proves nothing; check the PR for check runs instead. No CI → no gate; don't wait for checks that will never come.
4. `merge_pull_request`, `merge_method: squash`, title `<subject> (#<pr>)`. Merge directly — don't pre-read status; the call fails loudly if it isn't mergeable.
5. Sync local `main`: `git checkout main && git pull origin main`.

The two divergent points — **`squash`** as the method and **CI gating** — are exactly what a bespoke policy file changes.

## After the merge: capture the conversation (every session, every user)

Once the merge has landed and local `main` is synced, run the growth pack's capture step:
`node .claudinite/shared/packs/grow_with_claudinite/capture-log.mjs --issue <n>` (from the canon repo itself: `node packs/grow_with_claudinite/capture-log.mjs --issue <n>`), `<n>` being the issue your `Closes #<issue>` named. Deterministic, seconds-long; it pushes the conversation to the orphan `conversation-logs` branch, and the pack's **per-repo conversation-extract nightly** does the lessons extraction with a rethink window ([the pack README](../../packs/grow_with_claudinite/README.md) owns the standard). A later merge in the same session just runs it again — the step captures the delta. There is no in-session lessons pass anymore — extraction lives with the captured logs; in the rare repo that removed the `grow_with_claudinite` pack from `.claudinite-checks.json`, skip this step.

## Don't

- **Don't** re-read the issue to confirm it closed — `Closes #<issue>` does that on merge; trust it.

content.subject: Phase 1: codify pack-independence rule (check + ordering check-the-work test + docs)
content.description: New basics check: a pack's code may import only its own files and the always-vendored engine surface (checks/lib, packs//skills/ root machinery) — never another pack's code or canon-internal trees. Cross-pack abilities flow through declaration + configuration. Includes the check-the-work test verifying (on the branch commit log) that the rule commit precedes the fix commits, per the owner's ordering meta-rule; plus DESIGN/extending doc updates. Own commit.

content.subject: Phase 2: fix the cross-pack misuse via declaration + configuration
content.description: Barriers pack gains contributed-barriers ability (packs contribute fixed barrier rules as data via pack.mjs `contributes`); basics + product-wiki require barriers and contribute their isolation barriers as config, dropping the defineBarrier imports; move `under` to checks/lib; move migrationActive to checks/lib so chrome-extension-release stops importing migrations/registry.mjs; update stale docs. Own commit.

content.subject: Phase 3: relevant migration fix (vendor coherence + consumer note)
content.description: Drop the import-closure approach (rebuild branch from origin/main); vendor set now carries barriers via the requires closure. Add real-corpus regression test (basics/product-wiki vendor sets include packs/barriers, import-closed) and a computeVendorSet coherence validation that errors on escaping imports; dated migration note for consumers whose local packs composed on the removed defineBarrier export. Own commit. Then update PR #348 + issue #349.
